content.text: Paper-finder service is down. I'll use the arXiv API directly. Let me write a robust search+download helper.

input.command:
cd /workspaces/llms_don_t_do_much_tom_in_writ_20260714_234243_6b6ff0f4
source .venv/bin/activate
cat > arxiv_search.py << 'PYEOF'
import arxiv, json, sys
queries = sys.argv[1:]
client = arxiv.Client(page_size=25, delay_seconds=3, num_retries=3)
seen = {}
for q in queries:
    search = arxiv.Search(query=q, max_results=15, sort_by=arxiv.SortCriterion.Relevance)
    try:
        for r in client.results(search):
            aid = r.entry_id.split('/abs/')[-1]
            if aid in seen: continue
            seen[aid] = {
                "id": aid,
                "title": r.title.replace('\n',' ').strip(),
                "authors": [a.name for a in r.authors][:6],
                "year": r.published.year,
                "cats": r.categories,
                "abstract": r.summary.replace('\n',' ').strip(),
                "pdf": r.pdf_url,
                "query": q,
            }
    except Exception as e:
        print(f"# ERROR on '{q}': {e}", file=sys.stderr)
print(json.dumps(list(seen.values()), indent=1))
PYEOF
python arxiv_search.py \
  "theory of mind large language models" \
  "audience aware text generation language models" \
  "pragmatics rational speech acts language generation" \
  "reader aware personalized text generation LLM" \
  > arxiv_raw1.json 2>arxiv_err1.txt
python -c "import json;d=json.load(open('arxiv_raw1.json'));print(len(d),'papers');[print(p['id'],p['year'],p['title'][:80]) for p in d]"
echo "---ERRORS---"; cat arxiv_err1.txt

content.text: The arXiv relevance sort is noisy. Let me use the Semantic Scholar API, which ranks better by relevance and citations, with targeted queries.

input.command:
cd /workspaces/llms_don_t_do_much_tom_in_writ_20260714_234243_6b6ff0f4
source .venv/bin/activate
cat > s2_search.py << 'PYEOF'
import requests, json, sys, time
API="https://api.semanticscholar.org/graph/v1/paper/search"
FIELDS="title,year,authors,abstract,citationCount,externalIds,openAccessPdf,url"
queries = [
 "theory of mind large language models evaluation",
 "audience design pragmatics natural language generation",
 "rational speech acts pragmatic language generation neural",
 "reader-aware personalized text generation large language models",
 "perspective taking listener model text generation",
 "chain of thought writing quality large language models",
 "why AI generated text sounds robotic homogenization LLM writing",
 "pragmatic reasoning language models communication",
 "audience modeling reader simulation LLM",
 "theory of mind story generation narrative LLM",
]
out={}
for q in queries:
    try:
        r=requests.get(API, params={"query":q,"limit":15,"fields":FIELDS}, timeout=40)
        if r.status_code!=200:
            print(f"# {r.status_code} for {q}", file=sys.stderr); time.sleep(3); continue
        for p in r.json().get("data",[]) or []:
            pid=p["paperId"]
            if pid in out: continue
            ext=p.get("externalIds") or {}
            out[pid]={
              "title":p.get("title"),"year":p.get("year"),
              "authors":[a["name"] for a in (p.get("authors") or [])][:6],
              "cites":p.get("citationCount"),
              "arxiv":ext.get("ArXiv"),"doi":ext.get("DOI"),
              "oa_pdf":(p.get("openAccessPdf") or {}).get("url"),
              "url":p.get("url"),"abstract":p.get("abstract"),"query":q,
            }
    except Exception as e:
        print(f"# ERR {q}: {e}", file=sys.stderr)
    time.sleep(3)
json.dump(list(out.values()), open("s2_raw.json","w"), indent=1)
print(f"{len(out)} unique papers")
PYEOF
python s2_search.py 2>s2_err.txt
echo "---ERR---"; cat s2_err.txt

content.text: Rate-limited on most queries. Let me retry the remaining ones with longer delays.

input.command:
cd /workspaces/llms_don_t_do_much_tom_in_writ_20260714_234243_6b6ff0f4
source .venv/bin/activate
cat > s2_search2.py << 'PYEOF'
import requests, json, sys, time
API="https://api.semanticscholar.org/graph/v1/paper/search"
FIELDS="title,year,authors,abstract,citationCount,externalIds,openAccessPdf,url"
queries = [
 "rational speech acts pragmatic language generation",
 "reader-aware personalized text generation language models",
 "perspective taking listener model generation",
 "chain of thought writing quality language models",
 "AI generated text homogenization writing style",
 "pragmatic reasoning language models communication",
 "audience modeling reader simulation generation",
 "theory of mind narrative story generation",
 "human likeness LLM writing detection stylometry",
 "self-refine reflection improve text generation LLM",
]
out=json.load(open("s2_raw.json"))
seen={p["title"] for p in out}
for q in queries:
    ok=False
    for attempt in range(4):
        try:
            r=requests.get(API, params={"query":q,"limit":15,"fields":FIELDS}, timeout=40)
            if r.status_code==429:
                time.sleep(8); continue
            if r.status_code!=200:
                print(f"# {r.status_code} for {q}", file=sys.stderr); break
            for p in r.json().get("data",[]) or []:
                if p.get("title") in seen: continue
                seen.add(p.get("title"))
                ext=p.get("externalIds") or {}
                out.append({
                  "title":p.get("title"),"year":p.get("year"),
                  "authors":[a["name"] for a in (p.get("authors") or [])][:6],
                  "cites":p.get("citationCount"),
                  "arxiv":ext.get("ArXiv"),"doi":ext.get("DOI"),
                  "oa_pdf":(p.get("openAccessPdf") or {}).get("url"),
                  "url":p.get("url"),"abstract":p.get("abstract"),"query":q})
            ok=True; break
        except Exception as e:
            print(f"# ERR {q}: {e}", file=sys.stderr); time.sleep(5)
    if not ok: print(f"# FAILED {q}", file=sys.stderr)
    time.sleep(6)
json.dump(out, open("s2_raw.json","w"), indent=1)
print(f"{len(out)} total unique papers")
PYEOF
python s2_search2.py 2>s2_err2.txt
echo "---ERR---"; cat s2_err2.txt

Links: [{"title":"[2505.18497] The Pragmatic Mind of Machines: Tracing the Emergence of Pragmatic Competence in Large Language Models","url":"https://arxiv.org/abs/2505.18497"},{"title":"Large Language Models as Theory of Mind Aware Generative Agents with   Counterfactual Reflection","url":"https://arxiv.org/pdf/2501.15355"},{"title":"ToM-agent: Large Language Models as Theory of Mind Aware Generative Agents with Counterfactual Reflection","url":"https://arxiv.org/html/2501.15355v1"},{"title":"Towards Open-Ended Evaluation and Preference Tuning","url":"https://aclanthology.org/2024.emnlp-main.1258.pdf"},{"title":"Explore Theory of Mind: Program-guided adversarial data generation for   theory of mind reasoning","url":"https://arxiv.org/pdf/2412.12175"},{"title":"Towards A Holistic Landscape of Situated Theory of Mind in Large   Language Models","url":"https://arxiv.org/pdf/2310.19619"},{"title":"If an LLM could genuinely deduce the intellectual baseline of its user from provided documents or conversational context, it could eliminate boilerplate introductions, redundant explanations, and...","url":"https://p4sc4l.substack.com/p/if-an-llm-could-genuinely-deduce"},{"title":"Theory of Mind in Modern Large Language Models (LLMs) | by Greg Robison | Medium","url":"https://gregrobison.medium.com/theory-of-mind-in-modern-large-language-models-llms-985b604f3371"}]

Based on the search results, here are the key findings on audience-aware pragmatic text generation and LLMs' theory of mind capabilities:

## Pragmatic Competence in LLMs

Current large language models have demonstrated emerging capabilities in social intelligence tasks, including implicature resolution and theory-of-mind reasoning, both of which require substantial pragmatic understanding. However, how LLMs acquire this pragmatic competence throughout the training process remains poorly understood.

## Audience-Aware Adaptation

Research by Takmaz et al. explored "Speaking the language of your listener: Audience-aware adaptation via plug-and-play theory of mind." This work demonstrates approaches to enabling models to adapt their communication based on audience understanding.

## Current Limitations

Despite progress, there are significant gaps in LLMs' pragmatic abilities. Because they lack a functional Theory of Mind to execute genuine audience design, they cannot dynamically assess the optimal information density required by a specific user. Additionally, LLMs struggle with tasks like pragmatic implicatures (reading between the lines of what someone means) and other subtle social inferences.

## Deeper Layer Processing

The significant boost in pragmatic reasoning is tied to deeper layer representations in transformer-based models, analogous to human high-level thinking.

The research indicates that while LLMs are developing pragmatic capabilities, there remain substantial challenges in achieving truly audience-aware text generation that adapts to readers' knowledge and perspectives.

Links: [{"title":"Reasoning About Pragmatics with Neural Listeners and Speakers – arXiv Vanity","url":"https://www.arxiv-vanity.com/papers/1604.00562/"},{"title":"Learning in the Rational Speech Acts Model","url":"https://www.researchgate.net/publication/283247369_Learning_in_the_Rational_Speech_Acts_Model"},{"title":"Collaborative Rational Speech Act: Pragmatic Reasoning for Multi-Turn Dialog","url":"https://arxiv.org/html/2507.14063v2"},{"title":"Categorical Tools for Natural Language Processing","url":"https://arxiv.org/pdf/2212.06636"},{"title":"Pragmatic Reasoning improves LLM Code Generation","url":"https://arxiv.org/html/2502.15835v2"},{"title":"Rational Retrieval Acts: Leveraging Pragmatic Reasoning to Improve Sparse Retrieval","url":"https://arxiv.org/html/2505.03676v1"},{"title":"Pragmatics in Language Grounding: Phenomena, Tasks, and Modeling   Approaches","url":"https://arxiv.org/pdf/2211.08371"},{"title":"Reevaluating pragmatic reasoning in language games","url":"https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pmc/articles/PMC7968720/"},{"title":"[1510.06807] Learning in the Rational Speech Acts Model","url":"https://ar5iv.labs.arxiv.org/html/1510.06807"}]

I've found comprehensive information about the Rational Speech Acts model and its applications to pragmatic language generation with neural listeners. Here are the key findings:

## Rational Speech Acts (RSA) Framework

The Rational Speech Act (RSA) framework offers a principled foundation for modeling pragmatic reasoning as recursive social inference between speakers and listeners. The RSA model treats language use as a recursive process in which probabilistic speaker and listener agents reason about each other's intentions to enrich the literal semantics of their language along broadly Gricean lines.

## Neural Implementation

Neural models of RSA use a pair of neural "listener" and "speaker" models to ground language in the world, and actively reason about listener behavior when selecting utterances. RSA defines a recursive reasoning process where speakers and listeners model each other's goals and interpretations—a rational speaker chooses utterances using an embedded model of how the listener will interpret them, while a rational listener reasons counterfactually about why the speaker chose an observed utterance rather than alternatives.

## Applications and Performance

RSA has successfully captured phenomena such as reference, implicature, and vagueness, and powered applications from grounded captioning to controlled generation, including image captioning and controllable text generation. In human evaluations on a referring expression game, this approach succeeds 81% of the time, compared to 69% using existing techniques.

## Learning from Data

The model can be optimized as a trained statistical classifier that uses the intermediate agents of RSA as hidden layers of representation forming a non-linear activation function.

Links: [{"title":"Mr. Nicolo' Brandizzi | Author","url":"https://sciprofiles.com/profile/2508045?amp=&amp="},{"title":"Speaking the Language of Your Listener: Audience-Aware Adaptation via Plug-and-Play Theory of Mind - ACL Anthology","url":"https://aclanthology.org/2023.findings-acl.258/"},{"title":"Speaking the Language of Your Listener: Audience-Aware Adaptation via Plug-and-Play Theory of Mind - UCL Discovery","url":"https://discovery.ucl.ac.uk/id/eprint/10216483/"},{"title":"Computer Science May 2023","url":"https://arxiv.org/list/cs/2023-05?skip=9100&show=2000"},{"title":"Instruct Once, Chat Consistently in Multiple Rounds: An Efficient Tuning Framework for Dialogue","url":"https://arxiv.org/html/2402.06967v1"},{"title":"[2305.19933] Speaking the Language of Your Listener: Audience-Aware Adaptation via Plug-and-Play Theory of Mind","url":"https://arxiv.org/abs/2305.19933"},{"title":"Towards A Holistic Landscape of Situated Theory of Mind in Large   Language Models","url":"https://arxiv.org/pdf/2310.19619"},{"title":"ACL2023: Speaking the Language of Your Listener: Audience-Aware Adaptation via Plug-and-Play Theory of Mind","url":"https://virtual2023.aclweb.org/paper_P3948.html"},{"title":"Speaking the Language of Your Listener: Audience-Aware Adaptation via   Plug-and-Play Theory of Mind","url":"https://arxiv.org/pdf/2305.19933"},{"title":"Large Language Models as Theory of Mind Aware Generative Agents with   Counterfactual Reflection","url":"https://arxiv.org/pdf/2501.15355"}]

Based on the search results, I found the paper you're looking for:

**"Speaking the Language of Your Listener: Audience-Aware Adaptation via Plug-and-Play Theory of Mind"** by Ece Takmaz, Nicolo' Brandizzi, Mario Giulianelli, Sandro Pezzelle, and Raquel Fernandez, published in Findings of the Association for Computational Linguistics: ACL 2023.

**Overview:**
The paper addresses how dialogue participants may have varying levels of knowledge about topics under discussion, making it essential for speakers to adapt their utterances by considering their audience—an open question for modeling such adaptation in computational agents.

**Methodology:**
The research models a visually grounded referential game between a knowledgeable speaker and a listener with more limited visual and linguistic experience, endowing the speaker with the ability to adapt its referring expressions via a simulation module that monitors the effectiveness of planned utterances from the listener's perspective.

**Key Innovation:**
The authors propose an adaptation mechanism building on plug-and-play approaches to controlled language generation, where utterance generation is steered on the fly by the simulator without finetuning the speaker's underlying language model.

**Results:**
The approach is effective, with the speaker's utterances becoming closer to the listener's domain of expertise, leading to higher communicative success.

The paper was published on arXiv as preprint 2305.19933 in May 2023.

Links: [{"title":"(PDF) Homogenizing effect of large language models (LLMs) on creative diversity: An empirical comparison of human and ChatGPT writing","url":"https://www.researchgate.net/publication/395887697_Homogenizing_effect_of_large_language_models_LLMs_on_creative_diversity_An_empirical_comparison_of_human_and_ChatGPT_writing"},{"title":"Homogenizing Effect of a Large Language Model (LLM) on Creative Diversity: An Empirical Comparison of Human and ChatGPT Writing | Request PDF","url":"https://www.researchgate.net/publication/383466490_Homogenizing_Effect_of_Large_Language_Model_LLM_on_Creative_Diversity_An_Empirical_Comparison_of_Human_and_ChatGPT_Writing"},{"title":"Homogenizing effect of large language models (LLMs) on creative diversity: An empirical comparison of human and ChatGPT writing - ScienceDirect","url":"https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S294988212500091X"},{"title":"The Homogenization Problem in LLMs: Towards Meaningful Diversity in AI Safety","url":"https://arxiv.org/pdf/2601.06116"},{"title":"Writing in Symbiosis: Mapping Human Creative Agency in the AI Era","url":"https://arxiv.org/pdf/2512.13697"},{"title":"Exploring Language Patterns of Prompts in Text-to-Image Generation and   Their Impact on Visual Diversity","url":"https://arxiv.org/pdf/2504.14125"},{"title":"Epistemic Diversity and Knowledge Collapse in Large Language Models","url":"https://arxiv.org/pdf/2510.04226"},{"title":"Aspirational Affordances of AI","url":"https://arxiv.org/pdf/2504.15469"},{"title":"AI-Slop to AI-Polish? Aligning Language Models through Edit-Based Writing Rewards and Test-time Computation","url":"https://arxiv.org/pdf/2504.07532"}]

I found relevant research on this topic. Here are the key findings:

## Homogenization Effects and Creative Diversity

In three studies, human writing increased the collective semantic diversity of a group of essays approximately two to eight times more than base GPT-4 essays did. This homogenizing effect persisted even after a range of enhancements to the diversity of the GPT-4 writings, including prompt and parameter modifications.

## The Paradox of AI-Driven Homogenization

Research from 2024-2025 reveals an important paradox: writers exposed to GPT-4 generated ideas produced better and more enjoyable stories, however, these LLM-inspired stories were more similar to each other than stories written by humans alone. LLMs are designed to rely on probability distributions; consequently, the outputs of LLMs tend to lack surprise or novelty, as these models are fundamentally trained to conform to existing data distributions that are biased in favor of predominant cultural ideas.

## Cultural and Stylistic Homogenization

AI suggestions homogenize writing toward western styles: implications for diversity and cultural nuance. This indicates the homogenization issue extends beyond generic writing quality to cultural representation.

## Broader Implications

Even as AI tools continue to improve and produce ever better and more creative output, they may still contribute to an overall homogenization of ideas. Using LLMs may lead to better outcomes for individual creators but at the cost of these outcomes becoming more similar across creators.

The research suggests that while LLMs can produce individual outputs as creative as humans, the widespread adoption of these tools poses risks to collective creative diversity.

for aid, fn in PAPERS:
    path=os.path.join("papers",fn)
    if os.path.exists(path) and os.path.getsize(path)>10000:
        print("skip",fn); continue
    ok=False
    for url in [f"https://arxiv.org/pdf/{aid}.pdf", f"https://arxiv.org/pdf/{aid}"]:
        try:
            r=requests.get(url, headers=hdr, timeout=60)
            if r.status_code==200 and r.content[:4]==b"%PDF":
                open(path,"wb").write(r.content)
                print(f"OK {fn} ({len(r.content)//1024} KB)"); ok=True; break
        except Exception as e:
            print(f"err {aid}: {e}")
        time.sleep(2)
    if not ok: print(f"FAIL {aid}")
    time.sleep(2)
PYEOF
python download_papers.py 2>&1
echo "=== FILES ==="; ls -la papers/

content.text: All 14 papers downloaded. The user opted into workflows, and parallel deep-reading of these papers is an ideal fit. Let me launch a workflow to read all papers concurrently and return structured notes, then work on datasets/code while it runs.

input.script:
export const meta = {
  name: 'deep-read-tom-writing-papers',
  description: 'Deep-read 14 papers on ToM/audience-aware generation in parallel, return structured notes',
  phases: [{ title: 'Read', detail: 'one agent per paper PDF' }],
}

const NOTE_SCHEMA = {
  type: 'object',
  additionalProperties: false,
  required: ['title','year','key_contribution','methodology','datasets','baselines','metrics','key_results','code_link','relevance'],
  properties: {
    title: { type: 'string' },
    year: { type: 'string' },
    key_contribution: { type: 'string', description: '2-3 sentences' },
    methodology: { type: 'string', description: 'concrete approach/algorithm, 3-5 sentences' },
    datasets: { type: 'string', description: 'named datasets used with sizes if given' },
    baselines: { type: 'string' },
    metrics: { type: 'string' },
    key_results: { type: 'string', description: 'main quantitative/qualitative findings' },
    code_link: { type: 'string', description: 'github/project URL or "none found"' },
    relevance: { type: 'string', description: 'specific relevance to the hypothesis: does having an LLM simulate the audience in its chain-of-thought each sentence improve writing? 2-4 sentences' },
  }
}

const DIR = '/workspaces/llms_don_t_do_much_tom_in_writ_20260714_234243_6b6ff0f4/papers/'
const PAPERS = [
 '2305.19933_speaking_language_of_listener_audience_aware_ToM.pdf',
 '2301.10283_audience_centric_NLG_style_infusion.pdf',
 '2501.15355_ToM_agent_generative_agents_counterfactual_reflection.pdf',
 '2505.18497_pragmatic_mind_of_machines_emergence.pdf',
 '1604.00562_reasoning_about_pragmatics_neural_listeners_speakers.pdf',
 '2502.15835_pragmatic_reasoning_improves_llm_code_generation.pdf',
 '2302.08399_llms_fail_trivial_alterations_tom_tasks.pdf',
 '2305.14763_clever_hans_or_neural_tom_stress_testing.pdf',
 '2502.06470_survey_theory_of_mind_in_llms.pdf',
 '2407.06004_perceptions_to_beliefs_precursory_inferences_tom.pdf',
 '2504.07532_ai_slop_to_ai_polish_edit_based_writing_rewards.pdf',
 '2601.06116_homogenization_problem_in_llms_diversity.pdf',
 '2211.08371_pragmatics_in_language_grounding_survey.pdf',
 '2507.14063_collaborative_rational_speech_act_multiturn_dialog.pdf',
]

phase('Read')
const HYP = 'Hypothesis under study: one reason LLMs write annoyingly is they do not model the audience; having the model explicitly simulate the audience in its chain-of-thought every sentence or so may improve writing quality (a Theory-of-Mind / audience-design intervention).'

const results = await parallel(PAPERS.map(fn => () =>
  agent(
    `Read the research paper PDF at ${DIR}${fn} using the Read tool (it renders PDFs directly). Read enough of it (abstract, method, experiments, results) to extract accurate structured notes. ${HYP} Focus especially on what is concretely reusable for an experiment testing this hypothesis: datasets, tasks, baselines, evaluation metrics, and any code. Return the structured note.`,
    { label: fn.slice(0,25), phase: 'Read', schema: NOTE_SCHEMA }
  ).then(n => ({ file: fn, ...n }))
))

return results.filter(Boolean)

input.command:
cd /workspaces/llms_don_t_do_much_tom_in_writ_20260714_234243_6b6ff0f4
source .venv/bin/activate
# Query arxiv abstract pages for github links via the arxiv comment/abstract won't have them; use S2 / arxiv listing. Instead probe common repos.
python - << 'PYEOF'
import requests
# Semantic Scholar: get external repo / find github via arxiv abstract page
cands = {
 "2305.19933":"speaking language listener plug and play ToM",
 "2501.15355":"ToM-agent counterfactual reflection",
 "2502.15835":"pragmatic reasoning improves llm code generation",
 "2402.15052":"ToMBench",
 "2504.07532":"AI slop to AI polish",
}
hdr={"User-Agent":"Mozilla/5.0"}
for aid in cands:
    try:
        r=requests.get(f"https://arxiv.org/abs/{aid}", headers=hdr, timeout=30)
        txt=r.text
        import re
        gh=set(re.findall(r'https?://github\.com/[\w\-./]+', txt))
        print(aid, cands[aid], "->", list(gh)[:4] if gh else "no github in abs page")
    except Exception as e:
        print(aid,"ERR",e)
PYEOF

content.content:
Web search results for query: "FANToM benchmark theory of mind conversation github dataset huggingface"

Links: [{"title":"Explore Theory-of-Mind: Program-Guided Adversarial Data Generation for Theory of Mind Reasoning","url":"https://arxiv.org/html/2412.12175"},{"title":"OpenToM: A Comprehensive Benchmark for Evaluating Theory-of-Mind   Reasoning Capabilities of Large Language Models","url":"https://arxiv.org/pdf/2402.06044"},{"title":"Daily Papers - Hugging Face","url":"https://huggingface.co/papers?q=theory-of-mind+benchmarks"},{"title":"GitHub - skywalker023/thought-tracing: 🚲 Code and benchmark for our COLM 2025 paper - \"Thought Tracing: Hypothesis-Driven Theory-of-Mind Reasoning for Large Language Models\"","url":"https://github.com/skywalker023/thought-tracing"},{"title":"FANToM: A Benchmark for Stress-testing Machine Theory of Mind in Interactions - ACL Anthology","url":"https://aclanthology.org/2023.emnlp-main.890/"},{"title":"GitHub - skywalker023/fantom: 👻 Code and benchmark for our EMNLP 2023 paper - \"FANToM: A Benchmark for Stress-testing Machine Theory of Mind in Interactions\"","url":"https://github.com/skywalker023/fantom"},{"title":"REALTALK: A 21-Day Real-World Dataset for Long-Term Conversation","url":"https://arxiv.org/pdf/2502.13270"},{"title":"FANToM: A New Benchmark for Machine ToM in Interactions","url":"https://hyunw.kim/fantom/"},{"title":"[2310.15421] FANToM: A Benchmark for Stress-testing Machine Theory of Mind in Interactions","url":"https://arxiv.org/abs/2310.15421"}]

Based on the search results, here's what I found about FANToM:

## FANToM Benchmark Overview

FANToM is a new benchmark designed to stress-test Theory of Mind (ToM) within information-asymmetric conversational contexts via question answering.

## Key Characteristics

Rather than focusing on passive narratives that lack interactivity, FANToM tests models using interactive conversational contexts. The benchmark leverages information-asymmetry in conversational contexts where conversations continue during a character's absence, and the absent character later rejoins unaware of the previously shared information.

## Question Types

FANToM includes multiple types of questions: (1) BeliefQ (choice and free-response types), (2) AnswerabilityQ (list and binary types), and (3) InfoAccessQ (list and binary types).

## Performance and Results

FANToM is challenging for state-of-the-art LLMs, which perform significantly worse than humans even with chain-of-thought reasoning or fine-tuning.

## Availability

The benchmark was published at the 2023 EMNLP conference. Code is available on GitHub at skywalker023/fantom, and results from recent models such as GPT-4o, Gemini, Llama-3, Mixtral, and Claude Opus are available on their GitHub repository.

results:
Based on the search results, here's what I found about FANToM:

## FANToM Benchmark Overview

FANToM is a new benchmark designed to stress-test Theory of Mind (ToM) within information-asymmetric conversational contexts via question answering.

## Key Characteristics

Rather than focusing on passive narratives that lack interactivity, FANToM tests models using interactive conversational contexts. The benchmark leverages information-asymmetry in conversational contexts where conversations continue during a character's absence, and the absent character later rejoins unaware of the previously shared information.

## Question Types

FANToM includes multiple types of questions: (1) BeliefQ (choice and free-response types), (2) AnswerabilityQ (list and binary types), and (3) InfoAccessQ (list and binary types).

## Performance and Results

FANToM is challenging for state-of-the-art LLMs, which perform significantly worse than humans even with chain-of-thought reasoning or fine-tuning.

## Availability

The benchmark was published at the 2023 EMNLP conference. Code is available on GitHub at skywalker023/fantom, and results from recent models such as GPT-4o, Gemini, Llama-3, Mixtral, and Claude Opus are available on their GitHub repository.

Based on the search results, here's information about the ToMBench repository:

## ToMBench GitHub Repository

ToMBench is released at https://github.com/zhchen18/ToMBench.

### Overview
ToMBench is a systematic, automated, and original bilingual Theory of Mind (ToM) benchmark for LLMs, containing 2,860 testing samples involving diverse real-world social scenarios.

### Key Characteristics
ToMBench features a systematic evaluation framework encompassing 8 tasks and 31 abilities in social cognition, a multiple-choice question format to support automated and unbiased evaluation, and a build-from-scratch bilingual inventory to strictly avoid data leakage.

### Repository Contents
The inventory of ToMBench can be found in ToMBench_release_v1_0618.xlsx, and the jsonl version of ToMBench is available in the ./data folder.

### Publication
The paper was published at ACL 2024, with arXiv identifier 2402.15052.

### Important Note
ToMBench is designed for evaluation purposes only, and users should avoid using ToMBench data for training in any circumstance to prevent data contamination.

content.text: Repos cloned (all small, with reusable data). Now let me get a writing/audience generation dataset for the actual experiment. Let me install `datasets` and pull compact, on-point datasets from HuggingFace.

input.command:
cd /workspaces/llms_don_t_do_much_tom_in_writ_20260714_234243_6b6ff0f4
source .venv/bin/activate
uv pip install "datasets>=2.19" huggingface_hub 2>&1 | tail -2
python - << 'PYEOF'
# Probe candidate audience/writing datasets via HF datasets-server (no full download)
import requests
cands = [
 "rexarski/eli5_category",
 "euclaise/writingprompts",
 "nvidia/HelpSteer2",
 "microsoft/WritingBench",
]
for d in cands:
    try:
        r=requests.get("https://datasets-server.huggingface.co/info", params={"dataset":d}, timeout=30)
        ok = r.status_code==200
        print(d, r.status_code, "OK" if ok else r.text[:120])
    except Exception as e:
        print(d,"ERR",e)
PYEOF

content.text: The datasets-server proxy is flaky (503). Let me load directly via the `datasets` library with streaming to grab usable subsets.

input.command:
cd /workspaces/llms_don_t_do_much_tom_in_writ_20260714_234243_6b6ff0f4
source .venv/bin/activate
export HF_HUB_DISABLE_PROGRESS_BARS=1
python - << 'PYEOF'
from datasets import load_dataset
import json, os
os.makedirs("datasets/eli5_category_sample", exist_ok=True)
# ELI5-category: explanation for a lay audience (audience-adaptation signal)
try:
    ds = load_dataset("rexarski/eli5_category", split="train", streaming=True)
    rows=[]
    for i,ex in enumerate(ds):
        rows.append({k:ex[k] for k in ex if k in ("q_id","title","selftext","category","answers")})
        if len(rows)>=500: break
    json.dump(rows, open("datasets/eli5_category_sample/train_500.json","w"), indent=1, default=str)
    print("ELI5 sample rows:", len(rows), "| keys:", list(rows[0].keys()))
    print("example title:", rows[0].get("title"))
except Exception as e:
    print("ELI5 FAIL:", repr(e)[:300])
PYEOF

content.text: ELI5-category uses a deprecated script loader. Let me grab parquet-native datasets instead — WritingPrompts for open-ended writing.

input.command:
cd /workspaces/llms_don_t_do_much_tom_in_writ_20260714_234243_6b6ff0f4
source .venv/bin/activate
export HF_HUB_DISABLE_PROGRESS_BARS=1
python - << 'PYEOF'
from datasets import load_dataset
import json, os
def sample(name, split, keys, n, out, cfg=None):
    try:
        ds = load_dataset(name, cfg, split=split, streaming=True) if cfg else load_dataset(name, split=split, streaming=True)
        rows=[]
        for ex in ds:
            rows.append({k:ex.get(k) for k in keys} if keys else dict(ex))
            if len(rows)>=n: break
        os.makedirs(os.path.dirname(out), exist_ok=True)
        json.dump(rows, open(out,"w"), indent=1, default=str)
        print(f"OK {name}: {len(rows)} rows -> {out} | keys {list(rows[0].keys())}")
    except Exception as e:
        print(f"FAIL {name}: {repr(e)[:200]}")

sample("euclaise/writingprompts","train",["prompt","story"],300,"datasets/writingprompts_sample/train_300.json")
PYEOF
