<system-reminder>[Truncated: PARTIAL view — showing lines 1-147 of 189 total (27302 tokens, cap 25000). Call Read with offset=148 limit=147 for the next page, or Grep to find a specific section. Do NOT answer from this page alone if the answer may be further in the file.]</system-reminder>

1	{
2	  "summary": "Deep-read 14 papers on ToM/audience-aware generation in parallel, return structured notes",
3	  "agentCount": 14,
4	  "logs": [],
5	  "result": [
6	    {
7	      "file": "2305.19933_speaking_language_of_listener_audience_aware_ToM.pdf",
8	      "title": "Speaking the Language of Your Listener: Audience-Aware Adaptation via Plug-and-Play Theory of Mind",
9	      "year": "2023",
10	      "key_contribution": "Models audience-aware language adaptation in a visually grounded referential game where a knowledgeable speaker must adapt referring expressions to a listener with restricted (single-domain) knowledge. The key idea is to equip the speaker with a \"simulator\" module (a Theory-of-Mind / forward-prediction model of the listener) that predicts whether the listener would resolve a planned utterance, and to use its gradient to steer generation on the fly via a plug-and-play mechanism, without finetuning the frozen speaker LM. Shows audience-aware simulation raises listener resolution accuracy in both in-domain and out-of-domain settings.",
11	      "methodology": "Referential game: a frozen encoder-decoder (LSTM) speaker generates a referring utterance for a target image among 6 candidates; a frozen discriminator \"listener\" picks the referent. A separately trained \"simulator\" predicts the listener's choice from the visual context plus (a) the planned utterance text and (b) the decoder's initial hidden state h0 (the second stream is needed because argmax over text is non-differentiable, so gradients flow via h0). At inference an adaptation loop (Algorithm 1) runs: generate utterance, simulator predicts listener target, if wrong compute cross-entropy loss vs. gold target and backprop to update only h0 (Adam), regenerate; stop when the simulator predicts success or a max step count is reached. Random seed is reset each step to isolate h0 effects from sampling noise, and h0 is conditioned on the previous gold utterance to reduce language drift. Three settings compared: Baseline (no simulation), Self-aware (simulator models a general/all-domains listener, i.e. classic pragmatic self-monitoring), and Audience-aware (one simulator per domain-specific listener, i.e. a ToM of a less-knowledgeable listener). Models are trained from scratch (no pretrained LLMs) to fully control knowledge asymmetry.",
12	      "datasets": "PhotoBook dataset (Haber et al. 2019) of task-oriented visually grounded English dialogues; specifically the referring-utterance extraction of Takmaz et al. 2020 (~41,340 utterances, each paired with a target image + 5 distractors; Table 1 lists 45,741 utterances after domain splitting, |V|=6,038, 324 images). Images are from MS-COCO across 30 visual domains, clustered into 5 macro-domains with minimal vocabulary overlap: appliances, food, indoor, outdoor, vehicles. Split 70/15/15 train/val/test; also an \"all-domains\" merge. ResNet-152 image features.",
13	      "baselines": "Baseline = speaker with no simulation/adaptation (egocentric generation). Self-aware adaptation = simulation of a generic listener with the same (all-domains) knowledge as the speaker (equivalent to a pragmatic RSA-style self-monitoring speaker, but with predicted rather than observed listener responses). These two serve as the comparison points for the proposed audience-aware adaptation. Random-choice reference = 16.67% (1/6). Related-work baselines discussed but not directly compared: RSA framework, Greco et al. 2023 (RSA expert-to-layman), Singh et al. 2023 (listener subtraction).",
14	      "metrics": "Primary: listener resolution accuracy (communicative success), split into in-domain (IND) and out-of-domain (OOD); MRR for listeners. Simulator prediction accuracy. Speaker NLG quality: BLEU-1..4, ROUGE, CIDEr, BERTScore (P/R/F1). Analysis metrics: diagnostic probing accuracy (logistic regression predicting image-domain vs. listener-domain from h0 across adaptation steps), type-utterance ratio, type-token ratio, unigram POS distribution, Age-of-Acquisition (Kuperman et al. 2012), rate of lexical choice from target-image vs. listener-domain vocabulary.",
15	      "key_results": "Baseline: 52.30% IND / 19.06% OOD accuracy (OOD near random 16.67%). Self-aware adaptation: 65.09% IND but no OOD gain (19.11%). Audience-aware adaptation: 71.77% IND and 26.74% OOD — significant gains in both, especially closing part of the knowledge-asymmetry gap OOD. Probing shows adaptation replaces image-domain info in h0 with listener-domain info within ~3 gradient steps. Adapted vocabulary becomes more diverse (higher type-token ratio) but also more abstract (rising Age-of-Acquisition); successful adapted utterances use lower-AoA words, fewer target-image-domain words, and more listener-domain words. Some adapted utterances become disfluent/unnatural (no pretrained LM). Alternative stopping condition (adapt until the real listener guesses correctly) yields even higher accuracy.",
16	      "code_link": "https://github.com/nicofirst1/speaker-adaptation",
17	      "relevance": "Directly supports the audience-modeling hypothesis but via a decoding-time gradient mechanism rather than an LLM verbalizing audience simulation in chain-of-thought: it shows that explicitly predicting how the audience will react to a planned utterance, and revising accordingly, measurably improves communicative success (a concrete \"writing quality\" proxy = listener resolution). The self-aware vs. audience-aware contrast is a strong template for the experiment: modeling a specific audience (not a generic one) is what drives gains, arguing the intervention should condition on an actual reader model each sentence. Caveats for reuse: it is a constrained referring-game with a discriminative listener metric, uses trained-from-scratch LSTMs (not LLMs) and steers via backprop on hidden states rather than natural-language CoT; adaptation also risks disfluency/abstractness, echoing the \"annoying writing\" concern. Reusable assets: PhotoBook + Takmaz-2020 referring-expression data, the simulator-as-listener-model setup, and resolution-accuracy/AoA/vocabulary-overlap metrics."
18	    },
19	    {
20	      "file": "2301.10283_audience_centric_NLG_style_infusion.pdf",
21	      "title": "Audience-Centric Natural Language Generation via Style Infusion",
22	      "year": "2023",
23	      "key_contribution": "Proposes \"style infusion\": tethering the definition of subjective text style (persuasiveness, memorability) to the target audience via limited pairwise human comparisons rather than parallel corpora. Decouples a BERT style discriminator from a GPT-2 generator and infuses audience-preferred style adversarially, plus introduces an automatic style-transfer evaluation using a hierarchical Bayesian model of linguistic-feature correlations with audience preference.",
24	      "methodology": "Step 1: Train a BERT-based style discriminator (bert-base-uncased + FC layer, sentences concatenated with <SEP>) on pairwise audience judgments to predict which of two sentences is more persuasive/memorable. Step 2: Bootstrap/augment the small seed set using Universal Sentence Encoder to find top-k similar sentences in CNN/DailyMail, keeping pairs where the discriminator prefers the external candidate. Step 3: Infuse style into GPT-2 via adversarial training with a sample-dependent weighted loss L_SD = C*L_D + (1-C)*L_R, where L_R is reconstruction (MLE on the styled/preferred sample), L_D is a REINFORCE-style discriminator reward with a learned baseline reward, and C = beta*(1-alpha_S) with alpha_S = D(y*_s, y*_ns) making the style/fluency tradeoff sample-dependent. Note: this is a per-word/token RL reward from a learned scorer, NOT an explicit chain-of-thought audience simulation.",
25	      "datasets": "UKPConvArg1 (Habernal & Gurevych 2016): ~16,000 argument pairs over 16 non-overlapping topics, annotators pick the more persuasive argument (parallel: same topic/stance). Cornell Movie-Quotes Corpus (Danescu-Niculescu-Mizil 2012): ~2,200 paired movie quotes with crowdsourced memorability labels. CNN/DailyMail (See et al. 2017): 300,000+ news articles used only for bootstrapped data augmentation, not human-labeled.",
26	      "baselines": "(see baselines field)",
27	      "metrics": "(1) Percentage agreement with linguistic-feature correlations derived from a hierarchical Bayesian model (binomial/logit, NUTS MCMC) that correlates ~100 linguistic features (readability scores, dependency distances, Brunet index, type-token ratio, POS/NER rates, and Toubia et al. speed/volume/circuitousness) with audience preference; models scored by t-test significance of feature shifts vs pretrained GPT-2 (Tables 7-9). (2) ROUGE-1/2/L for on-topic relevance. (3) BERTScore F1 for semantic relevance. Note: no human evaluation of persuasiveness; style discriminator accuracy 89% (persuasion), 80% (memorability).",
28	      "key_results": "Discriminator: 89% accuracy on persuasiveness (87% on held-out topics), 80% on memorability. Best model (SD loss, beta=0.5 or 1.0 on augmented data) beats baselines on Bayesian feature agreement: persuasiveness up to 50.18% (SD-1.0) vs 29.51% (GPT-2), 44.01% (GPT2-Aug), 35.76% (TitleStylist); memorability 62.35% (SD-0.5) vs 40.71% (GPT-2). SD-0.5 also best on ROUGE (RG-L 0.3065 persuasion) with competitive BERTScore. Data augmentation drives most of the ROUGE/relevance gain; SD loss additionally corrects feature trends the data does not show and reduces neural degeneration. Higher beta (0.5-1.0) needed to avoid degeneration.",
29	      "code_link": "https://github.com/CrowdDynamicsLab/StyleInfusion (code and data released; trained generator checkpoint deliberately withheld due to dual-use concerns)",
30	      "relevance": "Directly relevant as a comparison point/foil: it targets the same failure mode (LLMs not writing in an audience-tailored way) but implements audience-modeling as an EXTERNAL learned reward/discriminator that scores each generation, not as the model simulating the audience in its own chain-of-thought. It thus offers a strong non-ToM, non-CoT baseline for the hypothesis and, crucially, reusable infrastructure: the UKPConvArg1 and Cornell Movie-Quotes pairwise-preference datasets and prompt sets, the TitleStylist/GPT-2 baselines, and especially the automatic evaluation suite (hierarchical Bayesian linguistic-feature correlations + BERTScore/ROUGE) which could quantify whether per-sentence audience simulation shifts generations toward audience-preferred features. It does not test any explicit ToM/audience-simulation-in-reasoning intervention, so it establishes the gap rather than filling it."
31	    },
32	    {
33	      "file": "2501.15355_ToM_agent_generative_agents_counterfactual_reflection.pdf",
34	      "title": "ToM-agent: Large Language Models as Theory of Mind Aware Generative Agents with Counterfactual Reflection",
35	      "year": "2025",
36	      "key_contribution": "First to apply Theory-of-Mind (ToM) modeling to open-domain conversational generative agents by disentangling \"belief\" from \"confidence\" (rather than binary true/false-belief). Introduces a counterfactual reflection mechanism where an agent predicts its counterpart's next utterance from inferred Belief-Desire-Intention (BDI) sets, then reflects on the gap between predicted and observed utterances to update inferred BDIs and confidences. Fully zero-shot (no training/annotation).",
37	      "methodology": "Two LLM-based agents converse: agent A generates utterances from its own (self-)BDIs; agent B tracks A's hidden BDIs. Three modules: (1) Self-BDI-aware module initializes A's BDIs zero-shot from a randomly sampled dialogue episode plus a \"reverse BDI argumentation\" step to push beliefs away from bland common sense; (2) Vanilla BDI tracking = infer top-1 BDI each turn and generate a reply (baseline); (3) CR-based tracking = infer top-k (k=3) BDIs with confidences summing to 100%, predict A's next utterance from the top-confidence BDI, score predicted-vs-real utterance similarity (0-1) via an embedding/similarity model, and run \"counterfactual reflection\" (what if the inferred BDI is wrong?) to produce an add/increase/decrease/delete/re-rank update plan over BDIs. A second-order-ToM judgment prompt lets A decide (SAY/GOODBYE) whether B has understood its BDI, which ends the episode (max 10 turns).",
38	      "datasets": "EmpatheticDialogues (Rashkin et al., 2019): 24,850 conversations, 32 emotion labels, splits 19,533/2,770/2,547 train/val/test, 810 participants, up to ~6-8 turns. Persuasion Dialogue / PersuasionForGood (Wang et al., 2019): 1,017 dialogues, 1,285 participants, 4,313 annotated persuasion-strategy instances across 10 strategy types (e.g., logical appeal, emotional appeal), donation task. BDI initialization done on 100 randomly sampled episodes per dataset; 100 conversation rounds evaluated.",
39	      "baselines": "\"Without ToM\" (plain generation, no mental-state modeling); \"Vanilla ToM\" (top-1 BDI tracking, no reflection); \"Reflection-based ToM\" (ToM-agent without counterfactual component); vs the full \"CR-based ToM\" (counterfactual reflection). GPT-4 vs GPT-3.5 compared as backbones. The Reflection-vs-CR comparison also functions as an ablation.",
40	      "metrics": "First- and second-order ToM BDI inference scored as binary similarity (human-annotator score 0-5, avg, threshold 0.25 for similar/not-similar) → Precision, F1, Recall reported separately for Belief, Desire, Intention. Downstream dialogue quality: Average Turn (AT, lower better) and Success Rate at turn t (SR@t, higher better), max 10 turns. Also cosine similarity between inferred and true BDI embeddings (text-embedding-3-large) tracked over turns to visualize confidence dynamics. 3 human annotators per pair.",
41	      "key_results": "CR-based ToM achieves the best downstream results on both datasets and both models. Empathetic/GPT-4: AT 5.80 and SR@t 0.55 for CR-based vs 6.53/0.43 without ToM. Persuasion/GPT-4: AT 5.25, SR@t 0.55 vs 5.51/0.45 without ToM. Ordering generally Without-ToM < Vanilla < Reflection < CR-based ToM. GPT-4 > GPT-3.5 for BDI inference. First-order and second-order ToM P/F/R generally improve with ToM+CR over Vanilla, though numbers are modest and noisy (many values 0.2-0.5, several exceptions). Confidence in inferred BDIs tends to rise and stabilize over a dialogue in \"good\" episodes.",
42	      "code_link": "No public code at time of writing; paper states \"codes will be made publicly available after acceptance\" (arXiv:2501.15355, no repo linked). None found.",
43	      "relevance": "Highly relevant conceptually but different in mechanics. It directly tests the hypothesis that having an LLM explicitly model the interlocutor's mental state improves conversational outcomes, and finds measurable gains (higher success rate, fewer turns) from audience/mental-state modeling. However, its \"audience simulation\" operates at the dialogue-turn level between two agents (infer partner BDIs, predict their reply, reflect), not per-sentence within a single writer's chain-of-thought, and it targets dialogue efficiency (AT/SR@t) rather than writing quality. Reusable for a writing-quality experiment: the BDI-inference and counterfactual \"predict-the-reader's-reaction then reflect\" prompt templates (given verbatim in the appendix), the AT/SR@t and human-similarity evaluation design, and the two named datasets. A per-sentence audience-simulation intervention could be seen as a finer-grained, single-agent adaptation of this predict-and-reflect loop, with its main missing piece being that the reader here is another live agent rather than an internal simulated audience."
44	    },
45	    {
46	      "file": "2505.18497_pragmatic_mind_of_machines_emergence.pdf",
47	      "title": "The Pragmatic Mind of Machines: Tracing the Emergence of Pragmatic Competence in Large Language Models (introduces the ALTPRAG dataset)",
48	      "year": "2025",
49	      "key_contribution": "First systematic analysis of how pragmatic competence in LLMs evolves across training stages (pretraining/base, SFT, and DPO preference optimization). Introduces ALTPRAG, a human-verified dataset built on the pragmatic notion of \"alternatives\": each item pairs two semantically equivalent but pragmatically distinct dialogue continuations, and asks a model to infer the speaker's intent and the context motivating each wording. Finds base models already have measurable pragmatic ability that scales with model size and pretraining-data volume, and that SFT (boosts cognitive-pragmatic intent inference) and DPO (boosts sociopragmatic/politeness sensitivity) each add gains.",
50	      "methodology": "The task: given a conversation context and two equally valid but pragmatically different candidate replies, the evaluated model must generate a free-form explanation of the speaker's underlying intent and why that particular wording was chosen (not a yes/no or multiple-choice label). Dataset construction: seed scenarios come from Hu et al. 2023 and the Sravanthi et al. 2024 benchmark; GPT-4o generates two contrastive continuations plus reference intent explanations; three pragmatics-trained annotators filter (majority pass), then a mirrored/swapped-order augmentation doubles the set. Evaluation uses LLM-as-a-judge (GPT-4.1) comparing model explanations against human-verified gold references, via (1) a 10-point scoring rubric per explanation and (2) pairwise win-rate between two model outputs, with the judge also classifying the winning explanation into cognitive-pragmatic / pragmalinguistic / sociopragmatic categories. Statistical significance of stage-to-stage gains is tested with the Wilcoxon test.",
51	      "datasets": "ALTPRAG: 1,300 examples (1,298 GPT-4o-generated -> 650 passed 3-annotator human filtering -> mirrored/swapped augmentation to 1,300). Each instance = context + initial utterance + two pragmatically distinct continuations + human-verified reference intent explanations. Built from Hu et al. (2023) scenario dataset and Sravanthi et al. (2024) pragmatic benchmark. Released on HuggingFace; code on GitHub.",
52	      "baselines": "Base (pretrained) models serve as the reference baseline against which SFT and DPO stages are compared. Model families/stages: OLMo-2 (7B, 13B, 32B; base/SFT/DPO via Tulu-3), OLMoE-1B-7B (MoE), LLaMA-3.1-Tulu-3 (8B, 70B; base/SFT/DPO), and Qwen-3 base models (0.6B, 1.7B, 4B, 8B, base only). Comparison is across training stages and model/data scales rather than against an external system.",
53	      "metrics": "(1) 10-point rubric score of each model explanation vs. gold reference (GPT-4.1 judge, with rationale); (2) pairwise win-rate (proportion of head-to-head wins by later-stage model); (3) win-category distribution over cognitive-pragmatic / pragmalinguistic / sociopragmatic. Judge validity checked via human-model agreement: Spearman rho ~0.65 (up to 0.85) on scoring, Cohen kappa ~0.56 on pairwise; inter-annotator Fleiss kappa (+-1 tolerance) reported.",
54	      "key_results": "Scores rise monotonically base -> SFT -> DPO, all gains statistically significant (Wilcoxon). Base 7-8B models already score ~6/10; DPO models reach 8+. DPO wins the highest pairwise win-rates in all comparisons (e.g., OLMo-2-7B: SFT-over-Base 73.1%, DPO-over-SFT 80.2%, DPO-over-Base 89.2%; Llama3-70B DPO-over-Base 89.0%). Pragmatic competence scales with model size (Llama-3.1 70B beats 8B 66% vs 34% on same corpus) and with pretraining data (Qwen-3 on 36T tokens; Qwen3-1.7B avg 6.48 > OLMo-2-7B 6.13). SFT gains are mostly cognitive-pragmatic (66.7% of OLMo-2-32B SFT wins); DPO adds sociopragmatic sensitivity.",
55	      "code_link": "Code on GitHub and dataset on HuggingFace (arXiv 2505.18497); exact URLs referenced via footnote but not spelled out in the extracted text.",
56	      "relevance": "Directly relevant as an evaluation harness rather than an intervention: ALTPRAG operationalizes \"audience design / speaker-intent inference\" as a concrete, scored task, so it can measure whether prompting a model to simulate the audience improves pragmatic reasoning. The 10-point rubric and pairwise win-rate LLM-as-judge protocol (GPT-4.1 vs. human gold intent explanations) is a ready-made, human-validated metric for an audience-modeling intervention. However, the paper tests interpretation (inferring others' intent), not generation quality; it does NOT test having a model simulate the audience in its chain-of-thought per sentence while writing, so it supplies the ToM/pragmatic measurement infrastructure and baselines but not the writing-improvement intervention itself."
57	    },
58	    {
59	      "file": "1604.00562_reasoning_about_pragmatics_neural_listeners_speakers.pdf",
60	      "title": "Reasoning about Pragmatics with Neural Listeners and Speakers",
61	      "year": "2016",
62	      "key_contribution": "A neural model that generates contrastive/pragmatic descriptions by combining a learned \"literal speaker\" (captioning model) and \"literal listener\" (retrieval model), then having a higher-order \"reasoning speaker\" (S1) explicitly select utterances that make the modeled listener behave correctly. It is the first learned captioning model to reason explicitly about listeners, and the first learned pragmatics approach requiring only ordinary (non-pragmatic) training data. This is a computational instantiation of Gricean/Rational-Speech-Acts audience design.",
63	      "methodology": "The task is a reference game: given a target referent r and a distractor r', produce a description that lets a listener pick the target. Base modules (referent encoder, description encoder, choice ranker, referent describer) are small feedforward nets trained on ordinary isolated captions. A literal listener L0 is trained contrastively (target vs. random distractor); a literal speaker S0 is a conditional neural language model. At inference, the reasoning speaker S1 samples n candidate descriptions from S0, then reranks each by pk = pS0(d|r)^lambda * pL0(target|d,r1,r2)^(1-lambda), selecting the argmax. The listener term rewards discriminativeness (audience will pick correctly); the speaker term regularizes toward fluent/human-like language, with lambda trading off fluency vs. informativeness (best at lambda=0.02, ~100 samples).",
64	      "datasets": "Abstract Scenes Dataset (Zitnick & Parikh 2013): 10,020 human-constructed clip-art scenes, each with up to 6 captions; scenes available as rendered images and as object/location feature vectors. Held-out dev and test sets of 1000 scenes each; from each, 200 paired (target, distractor) scenes were built in two conditions: \"All\" (up to 4 object differences) and \"Hard\" (exactly 1 difference). Prior pragmatics work used the smaller TUNA corpus of isolated object descriptions or small synthetic datasets.",
65	      "baselines": "Literal (S0): a standard image-captioning model trained normally on captions, ignoring context (context-blind). Contrastive: a reimplementation of Mao et al. 2015's soft-contrastive referring-expression model. Compiled (S1): a \"reflex\" direct model trained on captions sampled from the reasoning speaker (distills reasoning into a feedforward decoder, no inference at test). Also a hand-engineered pragmatic feature baseline (indicators only on distinguishing objects).",
66	      "metrics": "Accuracy: human success rate on the reference game (AMT raters shown two images plus a model description, must pick the matching image). Fluency: human 1-5 Likert linguistic-quality rating of isolated sentences. Significance via Binomial test (p<0.05). Also reports accuracy vs. number of samples and accuracy broken down by number of scene differences.",
67	      "key_results": "Reasoning speaker S1 reached 81% test accuracy (All) and 68% (Hard), vs. Contrastive 69%/58% and Literal 64%/53% — a 17% absolute gain on All and 15% on Hard over prior work; all differences significant. Dev accuracy: S1 83/73, Contrastive 71/54, Literal 66/54. Adding a tiny speaker weight (lambda=0.02) gave near-perfect fluency (4.8/5, matching the pure literal speaker) with almost no accuracy loss. Accuracy rose with samples (66% at 1 -> 83% at 100, plateauing). The \"compiled\" reflex model failed to recover reasoning gains (mean 69% vs 83%), indicating explicit inference-time reasoning about the listener is not easily distilled into a shallow feedforward net.",
68	      "code_link": "http://github.com/jacobandreas/pragma (models, human annotations, and code to regenerate all tables/figures)",
69	      "relevance": "Strong conceptual support for the hypothesis: performance improves substantially when the speaker explicitly simulates an audience/listener model and selects utterances that make that audience respond correctly, rather than generating context-blind (annoying/generic) text. Crucially, the audience-design signal is applied per-utterance via a rerank-by-simulated-listener step — directly analogous to having an LLM simulate the audience in its chain-of-thought before committing each sentence. The lambda trade-off is a concrete precedent for the fluency-vs-audience-informativeness tension a ToM intervention must balance. Reusable for an LLM experiment: the reference-game task, human accuracy + fluency metrics, and the context-blind captioner as a baseline map cleanly onto \"with vs. without simulated-audience CoT\" — though it uses tiny task-specific nets and a discriminative-listener rerank, not free-form LLM self-simulation, so the audience is a separate trained model rather than the generator reasoning about a reader in-context."
70	    },
71	    {
72	      "file": "2502.15835_pragmatic_reasoning_improves_llm_code_generation.pdf",
73	      "title": "Pragmatic Reasoning Improves LLM Code Generation (CodeRSA)",
74	      "year": "2026 (arXiv v5, May 2026; original v1 Feb 2025, arXiv:2502.15835)",
75	      "key_contribution": "Proposes CodeRSA, a Rational Speech Act (RSA)-inspired, execution-free reranker for natural-language-to-code generation. Its central idea is to model the listener/speaker: for each sampled code candidate it reverse-generates a \"candidate-induced instruction\" (a description of what the code actually does), then selects the candidate that the original instruction distinctively supports relative to these alternative interpretations. This operationalizes audience/alternative modeling (a pragmatics/ToM lens) as a tractable reranking signal, first work to apply RSA-style local pragmatic comparison to reranking LLM code candidates.",
76	      "methodology": "1) Sample n=10 code candidates for an instruction I0 (temperature 1.2, Python AST filter for validity). 2) For each candidate c_i, use the same base model (greedy decoding) to reverse-generate a candidate-induced instruction I_i describing what c_i does, forming an induced instruction set {I0,...,In}. 3) Compute literal-compatibility scores L0(I_j, c_i)=log P(c_i | I_j) for every instruction-candidate pair (raw sequence log-likelihood over code tokens). 4) Build local pairwise pragmatic contests: margin Δ_ij = [L0(I0,c_i)-L0(I_j,c_i)] - [L0(I0,c_j)-L0(I_i,c_j)], aggregated into a tournament-style win-rate (normalized Copeland score) \"pair_i\". 5) Add a global-support term avg_i = mean over instructions of log P(c_i | I_j) as a prior-like stabilizer. 6) Final score = z(pair_i)+z(avg_i) (within-task standardized, equal weight), pick argmax. No code execution is used.",
77	      "datasets": "HumanEval+ and MBPP+ (EvalPlus versions of HumanEval and MBPP with more rigorous test suites) and BigCodeBench (more complex instructions, richer function calls). Candidate pools of n=10 programs per problem; results averaged over 5 candidate-generation seeds (42-46). Manual analysis on 400 induced instructions; behavioral-diversity post-hoc analysis on 1000 pooled instances (200 per seed). Exact per-benchmark problem counts not stated in the read pages.",
78	      "baselines": "Random (expected accuracy of uniform selection from the pool), Coder (forward likelihood P(c|I0); Chen et al. 2021), CoderReviewer (bidirectional P(c|I0)*P(I0|c); Zhang et al. 2023), Consensus-WUCS (weighted inter-candidate unigram consensus + length-normalized Coder confidence; Jain et al. 2024). Also a finite vanilla RSA baseline (global normalization over the induced instruction set) in the appendix. Ablations: pair-only, avg-only components.",
79	      "metrics": "Final selection accuracy (pass@1 correctness of the single reranked pick, using functional-correctness test suites). Oracle@10-minus-Random \"candidate-pool headroom\" for context. Exact paired McNemar significance tests across 5 seeds (count of significant seeds). Manual annotation of induced instructions on behavioral faithfulness (Correct/Partial/Incorrect) and specificity (Specific/Somewhat generic/Too generic). Runtime (seconds per instance).",
80	      "key_results": "CodeRSA achieves the best average accuracy in 10 of 12 model-benchmark settings and outperforms Coder and CoderReviewer in all 12. Example gains over CoderReviewer: HumanEval+ Qwen2.5-7B 59.9 vs 52.0, Llama-3-70B 60.4 vs 55.6; MBPP+ Qwen2.5-32B-Coder +13.9 mean. Both components (pair, avg) are complementary and the full score beats either alone in all 12 settings; pair is usually the stronger single signal. CodeRSA helps most for smaller models and for behaviorally-diverse candidate pools (strongest in the top two diversity quartiles). Manual analysis: 70.3% of induced instructions behaviorally correct, 84.8% specific. Qualitative example shows CodeRSA picks the candidate faithful to task constraints (wins all 9 pairwise contests) while Coder/CoderReviewer pick an overly generic candidate. Limitation: cost grows quadratically in pool size (3.16s/instance vs 0.22s for Coder). Prompt-variation and cross-model sanity checks show robustness.",
81	      "code_link": "No code/GitHub URL is provided anywhere in the read pages (abstract, method, experiments, appendices). Base models and benchmarks are public: Llama-3-8B/70B, Qwen2.5-7B, Qwen2.5-32B-Coder; HumanEval+/MBPP+ (EvalPlus), BigCodeBench. code_link: none found.",
82	      "relevance": "Directly relevant as an existing operationalization of the ToM/audience-design hypothesis in a verifiable (code) domain: instead of narrating audience simulation in chain-of-thought, it makes the model explicitly generate the \"audience interpretation\" of each candidate (what a listener would infer the code does) and rewards outputs that are distinctively identifiable to the intended audience. This is a concrete, measurable proxy for \"modeling the audience,\" and its reverse-generation + pairwise-contrast machinery is reusable: for a writing experiment one could similarly have the model reverse-infer how readers would interpret each sentence/candidate and rerank or revise toward distinctively-supported phrasings. Caveat: it is applied per-candidate reranking (selection), not per-sentence in-line CoT audience simulation during generation, and it relies on execution-free likelihood signals unavailable for open-ended prose; its evaluation is functional-correctness accuracy, not human-judged writing quality, so metrics and human-eval design would need to be added."
83	    },
84	    {
85	      "file": "2302.08399_llms_fail_trivial_alterations_tom_tasks.pdf",
86	      "title": "Large Language Models Fail on Trivial Alterations to Theory-of-Mind Tasks",
87	      "year": "2023",
88	      "key_contribution": "A critique/position paper showing that GPT-3.5's apparent success on classic Theory-of-Mind (ToM) false-belief tasks (as reported by Kosinski 2023) is brittle: small, ToM-preserving perturbations of the vignettes flip the model's belief attributions to wrong answers. It argues model evaluation for intuitive psychology should adopt a skeptical zero-hypothesis in which outlying failures outweigh average success rates.",
89	      "methodology": "Using the exact same setup as Kosinski (posing a text vignette to GPT-3.5 and reading off the probabilities of alternative next-word completions for \"content\" and \"belief\" prompts), the author manually authors targeted perturbations of two classic false-belief tasks: Unexpected Contents (the \"Smarties\"/popcorn-vs-chocolate bag) and Unexpected Transfer (Sally-Anne, John/Mark/cat). Each perturbation preserves ToM logic but should change the correct belief attribution: making the container transparent (perceptual access), making the label uninformative (Sam cannot read), adding trusted testimony that contradicts the label, moving when the label is read to the end / having the protagonist fill and label the bag themselves, changing spatial relation \"in\" to \"on,\" and querying the mental state of the mover (Mark) rather than the naive agent. For each variant the model's completion probabilities (e.g., P(popcorn) vs P(chocolate)) are recorded to show the model still gives the \"false-belief\" answer even when it is now incorrect. No fine-tuning, no aggregate benchmark, no statistical testing; the analysis is a hand-crafted adversarial probe set.",
90	      "datasets": "No released dataset. Uses ~8 hand-written vignette variants derived from the two tasks in Kosinski (2023): Unexpected Contents variations 1A Transparent Access, 1B Uninformative Label (cannot read), 1C Trusted/Trustworthy Testimony, 1D Late Labels; Unexpected Transfer variations 2A Transparent Access, 2B Relationship Change (in->on), 2C Trusted Communication, 2D Querying the Other Person (Mark). Original Kosinski materials/methods are noted as publicly available. Illustrative figures generated with DALL-E 2 but images were not evaluated.",
91	      "baselines": "The implicit baseline is Kosinski (2023) \"Theory of Mind May Have Spontaneously Emerged in LLMs,\" reporting near-100% GPT-3.5 accuracy on the unperturbed tasks. Related pessimistic prior work: Sap et al. 2022 (Neural ToM limits). No quantitative baseline comparison is run; the paper contrasts unperturbed vs perturbed prompt completions on the same GPT-3.5 model.",
92	      "metrics": "Next-token/completion probabilities assigned by GPT-3.5 to the two competing continuations (e.g., P(popcorn) vs P(chocolate); P(box) vs P(basket)/P(chest)) for a \"content prompt\" and two \"belief prompts\" (\"She/He believes the bag/cat is...\" and a second desire-framed prompt \"She is delighted... loves eating...\"). Correctness is judged qualitatively as whether the model's high-probability completion matches the ToM-correct answer.",
93	      "key_results": "Every ToM-preserving perturbation flipped GPT-3.5 to the wrong belief attribution. Unexpected Contents: 1A transparent P(chocolate)=95%, 1B cannot-read P(chocolate)=98%, 1C trusted testimony P(chocolate)=97%, 1D self-filled/self-labeled P(chocolate)=87% — all should be \"popcorn.\" Unexpected Transfer: 2A transparent chest P(chest)=94%, 2B in->on P(basket)=97%, 2C trusted communication P(basket)=97%, 2D asking about the mover Mark P(basket)=99% (Mark himself moved the cat, so correct answer is box). The model was also shown sensitive to spurious cues (e.g., a double space changing the answer). Conclusion: ToM has probably not spontaneously emerged; the author advocates integrating structured cognitive ToM models with LLMs and warns that publishing a systematic variation generator/benchmark would let LLMs \"gobble up\" data to pass without genuinely learning ToM.",
94	      "code_link": "none found (author notes Kosinski 2023's materials are public but provides no repository for this paper)",
95	      "relevance": "Only indirectly relevant to the audience-simulation-in-chain-of-thought hypothesis. It provides no writing task, no CoT intervention, and no evidence that prompting a model to simulate another mind improves output — in fact it shows the opposite failure mode: default GPT-3.5 does NOT robustly model another agent's belief state, which motivates why an explicit audience-modeling step might help. It offers reusable false-belief probe templates and the completion-probability evaluation paradigm that could be adapted to test whether an explicit \"simulate the reader\" CoT step improves ToM-sensitive reasoning, but it is a diagnostic/critique paper, not an intervention or writing-quality study."
96	    },
97	    {
98	      "file": "2305.14763_clever_hans_or_neural_tom_stress_testing.pdf",
99	      "title": "Clever Hans or Neural Theory of Mind? Stress Testing Social Reasoning in Large Language Models",
100	      "year": "2023",
101	      "key_contribution": "A stress-test of whether LLMs possess robust Neural Theory-of-Mind (N-ToM), spanning 6 ToM tasks and 15 LLMs in zero-shot. Introduces two new evaluation resources (Adv-CSFB, an adversarial commonsense false-belief set built on Kosinski/Ullman, and ToMi', a sentence-completion adaptation of ToMi) and shows LLM ToM performance is brittle, dropping sharply on adversarial variants, evidence of shallow heuristics rather than genuine ToM.",
102	      "methodology": "The authors evaluate 15 LLMs (FlanT5 family, FlanUL2, GPT-3 davinci-002/003, GPT-3.5-turbo, GPT-4, Jurassic-2 family) zero-shot across 6 ToM datasets, comparing to a most-frequent-class (MFC) baseline. They compare three probing methods: LM-probing (pick highest-probability option), MC-probing (present context+question+choices, model emits the letter), and CoT-probing (reason step-by-step then answer). To test robustness they build Adv-CSFB by taking Kosinski's false-belief items and adding true-belief controls plus adversarial perturbations from Ullman (transparent access, uninformative label, trusted testimony, late labels, in-to-on, other person), then measure accuracy per adversarial category on belief-focused questions. They also stratify existing datasets (ToMi by question type, SocialIQa by main-character vs. others) to expose difficulty gaps hidden by aggregate scores.",
103	      "datasets": "TriangleCOPA (100 items); SocialIQa (400 sampled from 37,588); ToMi (400 sampled from 1000+ synthetic false-belief stories, memory/reality/first-&second-order); ToMi' (this paper: 30 stories x 6 = 180 questions, ToMi converted to sentence-completion format); Epistemic Reasoning from BIG-bench (2000 NLI items with factive/non-factive verbs); Adv-CSFB (this paper: 183 questions / 40 stories; 110 unexpected-contents + 73 unexpected-transfer, split into false-belief, true-belief, adversarial); FauxPas-EAI (176 questions / 44 stories, 22 faux-pas + 22 control, 4 questions each). Code and data at github.com/salavi/Clever_Hans_or_N-ToM.",
104	      "baselines": "Most-Frequent-Class (MFC) baseline per dataset (the primary sanity baseline). Cross-model comparison across 15 LLMs of varying size/family. Comparison across probing methods (LM-, MC-, CoT-probing) treated as internal baselines against each other. Comparison of ToMi' vs. ToM-k (Kosinski) to test spurious correlations; replication of Kosinski (2023) and Ullman (2023) prior results.",
105	      "metrics": "Accuracy (per-dataset, per-question-type, per-adversarial-category). Question-level accuracy and story-level \"joint score\" (all questions in a story must be correct) for FauxPas-EAI, Adv-CSFB, and ToMi'. Average-across-splits and lowest-split-score recommended as a robustness/difficulty metric. Entailment accuracy for Epistemic Reasoning. T-test used for the data-contamination analysis on SocialIQa standard vs. secret test set.",
106	      "key_results": "No model robustly exhibits ToM across all 6 datasets. Some datasets are near-solved (TriangleCOPA 96% flan-t5-xxl; GPT-4 97% on original false-belief items) while others fail badly (GPT-4 only 27% story-level on FauxPas-EAI, below MFC). On Adv-CSFB, adversarial perturbations collapse performance: e.g., GPT-4 drops to 0% on transparent-access and in-to-on transfer while scoring 97.5% on plain false belief. ToMi' scores far below ToM-k (GPT-4: 20% all / 52% no-second-order vs. 87% on ToM-k), indicating reliance on Sally-Anne pattern matching. Probing method matters (CoT vs. MC differ up to 7% on SocialIQa). Stratified splits reveal hidden weaknesses (GPT-3.5 drops from 81% to 46% on ToMi false-belief subset; GPT-4 from 79% to 74.5% on SocialIQa \"others\"). Newer instruction-tuned models often do WORSE on adversarial items, attributed to reporting bias and over-cooperativeness (treating every mentioned detail as relevant).",
107	      "code_link": "https://github.com/salavi/Clever_Hans_or_N-ToM",
108	      "relevance": "Only indirectly relevant. This paper is about whether LLMs can *answer questions about* others' mental states, not about whether prompting a model to *simulate the audience while writing* improves writing quality. It does not test audience-design or generation quality; its tasks are multiple-choice/completion comprehension. However, it is highly useful as cautionary methodology for any ToM-intervention experiment: (1) its finding that CoT can \"excessively guide\" models by leaking task structure warns that a per-sentence audience-simulation CoT could inflate results without genuine modeling; (2) its adversarial/true-belief control design and joint-score metric offer a template for verifying an intervention gives real gains rather than shortcut gains; (3) it shows LLMs' modeling of others' beliefs is brittle, which motivates but also tempers the hypothesis that models spontaneously model an audience."
109	    },
110	    {
111	      "file": "2502.06470_survey_theory_of_mind_in_llms.pdf",
112	      "title": "A Survey of Theory of Mind in Large Language Models: Evaluations, Representations, and Safety Risks",
113	      "year": "2025",
114	      "key_contribution": "A short survey/position paper (AAAI 2025, Apart Research) that synthesizes the literature on Theory of Mind (ToM) in LLMs. Its three claims are: (1) LLMs can match human performance on some specific ToM tasks but ToM remains limited and non-robust; (2) LLMs contain internal, linearly-decodable representations of self/other belief states that causally affect ToM performance; (3) advanced ToM introduces safety risks (privacy inference, deception, anthropomorphism, multi-agent collusion/escalation). It also recommends future evaluation directions beyond QA benchmarks toward authentic deployment settings.",
115	      "methodology": "This is a literature survey with no new algorithm, model, or experiment. It organizes prior work into behavioural ToM evaluation (QA benchmarks and psychological tests), representational/interpretability ToM (linear probes on residual-stream activations that extract belief states, and activation steering that causally changes false-belief answers), sources of future capability gains (scaling, prompting such as perspective-taking, test-time compute, scaffolding), and a taxonomy of safety risks split into user-facing (privacy/social engineering, deceptive behaviour, unintentional anthropomorphism) and multi-agent (exploitation, conflict escalation, collective misalignment). Mitigation strategies discussed include model unlearning, activation/representation engineering, and latent adversarial training. Note: the survey frames ToM as belief attribution and social reasoning, not as writing/audience-design.",
116	      "datasets": "No new dataset. It cites (does not run) ToM benchmarks reusable elsewhere: BigToM (Gandhi et al. 2023), FANToM (Kim et al. 2023, interaction stress-test), OpenToM (Xu et al. 2024), Hi-ToM (Wu et al. 2023, higher-order ToM), ToMBench (Chen et al. 2024b, ACL). Also references human-comparison test sets from van Duijn et al. 2023 (children aged 7-10), Strachan et al. 2024 (Nature Human Behaviour), and adversarial/stress sets from Shapira et al. 2024 and Ullman 2023. No sizes reported.",
117	      "baselines": "None run by the paper. Models discussed in cited works include GPT-4 and (via probing work) Pythia-70m and other open models. Perspective-taking prompting (Wilf et al. 2024, \"Think Twice\") is cited as a prompting intervention that improves ToM.",
118	      "metrics": "No metrics computed. Cited results use QA accuracy on ToM benchmarks (e.g., false-belief, irony detection, k-th order ToM accuracy), linear-probe decoding accuracy for belief states (near-perfect internal probing; ~80% transfer to real human conversations in Chen et al. 2024a), and human-vs-LLM comparison scores.",
119	      "key_results": "Qualitative/survey findings: LLMs (GPT-4) reach 7-10 year-old-child to adult-human level on some standard ToM tasks and can outperform humans on 6th-order ToM (Street et al. 2024), yet fail on hard/adversarial benchmarks (BigToM, FANToM, OpenToM, Hi-ToM, ToMBench) and trivial task alterations, indicating non-robust ToM. Linear probes recover others' belief states and steering with them significantly changes false-belief answers; probing accuracy rises with model size and fine-tuning. The paper argues current ToM QA benchmarks suffer from contamination/overfitting and calls for evaluation in authentic deployment scenarios (personal assistants, multi-agent environments, simulated social platforms).",
120	      "code_link": "none found (survey paper; no released code or data)",
121	      "relevance": "Only tangentially relevant. The paper supports the premise's foundation-that LLMs have (limited, non-robust) capacity to model others' mental states and can be prompted into better perspective-taking (Wilf et al. 2024, \"Think Twice\")-but it never studies writing quality or audience design, and offers no dataset, metric, or baseline for judging whether per-sentence audience simulation in chain-of-thought improves text. For the hypothesis, its concrete reuse is limited to pointers: the cited perspective-taking prompting method as a prior for the intervention, and its critique that ToM should be tested in realistic generation/deployment settings rather than QA. To test the writing hypothesis one would still need external writing-quality benchmarks and human/LLM-judge evaluations not provided here."
122	    },
123	    {
124	      "file": "2407.06004_perceptions_to_beliefs_precursory_inferences_tom.pdf",
125	      "title": "Perceptions to Beliefs: Exploring Precursory Inferences for Theory of Mind in Large Language Models",
126	      "year": "2024",
127	      "key_contribution": "Decomposes LLM theory-of-mind into two developmental precursors: perception inference (who saw/heard each piece of info) and perception-to-belief inference (deriving beliefs from those perceptions). Introduces two perception-annotated benchmarks (Percept-ToMi, Percept-FANToM) and a training-free framework, PercepToM, that has the LLM infer per-sentence/per-utterance perceivers, string-match to extract only the target character's perceived context, then answer the ToM question on that isolated context.",
128	      "methodology": "The core intervention (PercepToM) is a 3-step pipeline: (1) the LLM is prompted to output a JSON array mapping each sentence/utterance in the context to the set of characters who perceived it (perception inference); (2) a deterministic string-matching step extracts the \"perspective context\" — the subset of the input actually perceived by the target character; (3) the LLM answers the ToM question conditioned only on that filtered perspective context. This converts a false-belief scenario into an information-symmetric true-belief scenario. A key diagnostic finding: even when handed ground-truth perception annotations, LLMs still fail at belief inference because they cannot suppress irrelevant (non-perceived) information — a deficit the authors term weak \"inhibitory control.\" Explicitly separating perception tracking from the answer, and physically removing unperceived context, is what drives gains.",
129	      "datasets": "Percept-ToMi (extends Fixed & Disambiguated ToMi; 300 true-belief + 300 false-belief contexts, ~1802/1804 questions each, perceivers auto-annotated via SymbolicToM then hand-corrected) and Percept-FANToM (extends FANToM multi-party conversations; 340 true-belief + 539 false-belief contexts, 13161/25279 questions, perceivers manually annotated by join/leave times). Source: ToMi (first/second-order true/false belief QA) and FANToM (multi-party dialogue). Released CC BY-NC 4.0.",
130	      "baselines": "Vanilla (direct answer), Chain-of-Thought (\"Let's think step by step\"), System 2 Attention (S2A, LLM extracts relevant context first), SymbolicToM (graph-based belief tracker, ToMi only), and SimToM (perspective-taking prompt). PercepToM and a PercepToM+Oracle (ground-truth perceptions) ablation.",
131	      "metrics": "ToMi: accuracy = ratio of correctly answered story-question pairs. FANToM: set:ALL score (must answer all 5 ToM question types correctly for the same info). Perception inference accuracy = ratio of info units with correctly identified perceivers, averaged over contexts. Pearson correlation between ToM and precursor performance.",
132	      "key_results": "LLMs are strong at perception inference (avg acc 0.781 ToMi, 0.926 FANToM) but weak at perception-to-belief inference, especially false-belief. PercepToM beats Vanilla/CoT/S2A in most settings: GPT-4 Turbo reaches 1.000 on ToMi false-belief; GPT-4o rises from ~0.017 (vanilla) to 0.566 on FANToM false-belief; Llama-3-70B from ~0 to 0.147. Ablation: PercepToM+Oracle >> plain perception-to-belief, showing removing irrelevant info (inhibitory control) is the key lever. Perception inference accuracy correlates weakly/none with ToM; perception-to-belief correlates positively (r up to ~0.68).",
133	      "code_link": "No explicit GitHub URL in the paper; authors state benchmarks will be released publicly (CC BY-NC 4.0). arXiv:2407.06004. Code link: none found in text.",
134	      "relevance": "Moderately relevant. The paper's central mechanism — prompting the LLM to explicitly model, per sentence/utterance, whose perspective perceives each piece of content, then conditioning the answer on that mental-state model — is a direct instantiation of \"simulate the audience in the chain-of-thought each sentence\" for a comprehension task. It provides a concrete per-unit ToM-annotation format, prompts (Appendix C/D), and the finding that explicit audience/perspective modeling improves output, plus baselines (CoT, S2A, SimToM) reusable as comparison conditions. Caveat: it targets reading-comprehension QA, not open-ended writing quality, so it supports the plausibility of the intervention but does not test writing-quality outcomes directly."
135	    },
136	    {
137	      "file": "2504.07532_ai_slop_to_ai_polish_edit_based_writing_rewards.pdf",
138	      "title": "AI-Slop to AI-Polish? Aligning Language Models through Edit-Based Writing Rewards and Test-time Computation",
139	      "year": "2025 (COLM 2025; arXiv 2504.07532v3, Aug 2025)",
140	      "key_contribution": "Introduces (1) the Writing Quality Benchmark (WQ), consolidating five writing-preference datasets into 4,729 pairwise judgments where SOTA LLMs barely beat chance; (2) Writing Quality Reward Models (WQRM) trained on implicit preferences from expert edits (the LAMP corpus) that reach 74% accuracy and beat GPT-4o/o1/DeepSeek-R1/Skywork; and (3) an edit-based, three-step editing pipeline plus best-of-N test-time selection validated by 9 professional writers.",
141	      "methodology": "The core signal is implicit preference from expert edits: the LAMP corpus (AI-generated paragraph + MFA-expert-edited version, with 1-10 quality scores) is transformed into pairwise (P), scalar-regression (R), and combined (PR) training points. Reward models are trained in two families: ModernBERT-Large encoders (regression head with scaled sigmoid to 1-10, aparametric cosine-similarity classification head) and generative Llama3.1-70B via QLoRA outputting JSON preferences/scores. Generative variants are augmented with LLM-generated (Claude 3.5) \"silver rationales\" (contrastive for P, absolute critique for R, citing excerpts), used either on input (IR->O) or as output (I->RO); rationales are training-only, not available at test time. A separate three-step CoT editing model (SFT of GPT-4o/Llama3.1-70B on LAMP edit traces) detects idiosyncratic spans, rewrites each as an executable string-replace edit, and executes them; best-of-N=20 candidates are scored by WQRM and the top one selected.",
142	      "datasets": "LAMP (Language model Authored, Manually Polished): 1,282 <AI-generated, expert-edited> paragraph pairs (150-400 words, fiction/non-fiction), ~8 edits/passage, split 1,000 train / 67 val / 215 test; transformed into 2,000 (P), 2,000 (R), 4,000 (PR) training points. WQ Benchmark (4,729 pairs total) = Art or Artifice (144, flash fiction 1.5-3k words, human+GPT-3.5/GPT-4/Claude), LAMP-test (1,206), Style Mimic (300; MFA-student vs award-winning-author, Human-Human), Synthetic Mirror (1,120; author vs GPT-4o/Claude-3.5 mirror), LM Arena (1,959; crowd-sourced creative-writing pairs). Validation: 100 drafts (50 GPT-4o, 50 Llama3.1-70B) over 90 instructions across fiction/non-fiction/marketing; 300 expert triplet rankings. Content experiment: 50 Style Mimic instructions in 30-word vs ~180-word variants. Extra calibration data: 100 award-author paragraphs (score 10) + 80 MFA-published paragraphs (score 7.5).",
143	      "baselines": "Zero-shot/few-shot LLMs: GPT-4o, GPT-4o (5-shot), O1, O3, DeepSeek-R1; generic reward model Skywork-Reward-8B-v0.2 (top on RewardBench); Pangram AI-detector; and their own Llama3.1-70B WQRM variants vs ModernBERT WQRM variants. Editing-pipeline validation baselines: First Draft, Random Edit, Best Edit (WQRM-selected). Writing-quality profiling across GPT-4.5/4o/4o-mini/3.5, Claude 3.7/3.5/3-Haiku, Gemini 2.5-Pro/2.0-Flash/1.5-Pro/1.5-Flash, vs award-winning authors and MFA students.",
144	      "metrics": "Pairwise preference accuracy on WQ (per-dataset + overall; random = 50%); reward-model calibration (WQRM score gap vs expert-agreement %); sensitivity (WQRM score change per applied edit); expert human evaluation via triplet ranking (average rank, top-rank %, and overall/high-gap agreement between WQRM Best Edit and expert majority).",
145	      "key_results": "Zero-shot LLMs, including reasoning models (O1, R1), performed near random on WQ (GPT-4o 40.9%, O1 51.7%, R1 46.0%); out-of-the-box CoT reasoning did NOT help writing-quality assessment. GPT-4o few-shot rose 40.9->54.3 but no further gains. Best WQRM (MBERT-WQRM-PR) hit 74.3% overall (beating baselines ~10%+). Rationale augmentation (IR->O) improved generative models. WQRM is well-calibrated (agreement rises 55%->80% as score gap grows) and sensitive (+0.2 median score per edit). Best Edit ranked first overall (avg rank 1.58 vs 2.09 random-edit, 2.26 first-draft); experts agreed with WQRM 66% overall, 72.2% when gap>1. Even with detailed content, LLMs (~7.0 median) trailed MFA students (8.6) and award authors (8.9).",
146	      "code_link": "https://github.com/salesforce/creativity_eval (datasets and models released)",
147	      "relevance": "Directly relevant as a rigorous testbed and negative-baseline for the ToM/audience-design hypothesis: the paper shows generic chain-of-thought reasoning (O1/R1/O3) does NOT improve writing-quality assessment, and that per-sentence-style span-level self-editing (detect/rewrite/execute) plus best-of-N does help — offering a concrete comparison point for whether audience-simulation CoT beats plain CoT. Its WQRM reward model, WQ benchmark, LAMP edit corpus, editing pipeline, and 3-domain expert-ranking protocol are all reusable to score whether an audience-simulating writer produces higher-quality text. However, its notion of quality is intrinsic-craft (idiosyncrasies/cliches), not explicit reader-modeling, so audience-design would be a novel intervention layered on this evaluation scaffold rather than something the paper itself tests."

file.content:
{
  "summary": "Deep-read 14 papers on ToM/audience-aware generation in parallel, return structured notes",
  "agentCount": 14,
  "logs": [],

      "title": "Speaking the Language of Your Listener: Audience-Aware Adaptation via Plug-and-Play Theory of Mind",

      "key_contribution": "Models audience-aware language adaptation in a visually grounded referential game where a knowledgeable speaker must adapt referring expressions to a listener with restricted (single-domain) knowledge. The key idea is to equip the speaker with a \"simulator\" module (a Theory-of-Mind / forward-prediction model of the listener) that predicts whether the listener would resolve a planned utterance, and to use its gradient to steer generation on the fly via a plug-and-play mechanism, without finetuning the frozen speaker LM.       "methodology": "Referential game: a frozen encoder-decoder (LSTM) speaker generates a referring utterance for a target image among 6 candidates; a frozen discriminator \"listener\" picks the referent. A separately trained \"simulator\" predicts the listener's choice from the visual context plus (a) the planned utterance text and (b) the decoder's initial hidden state h0 (the second stream is needed because argmax over text is non-differentiable, so gradients flow via h0). At inference an adaptation loop (Algorithm 1) runs: generate utterance, simulator predicts listener target, if wrong compute cross-entropy loss vs. gold target and backprop to update only h0 (Adam), regenerate; stop when the simulator predicts success or a max step count is reached. Random seed is reset each step to isolate h0 effects from sampling noise, and h0 is conditioned on the previous gold utterance to reduce language drift. Three settings compared: Baseline (no simulation), Self-aware (simulator models a general/all-domains listener, i.e. classic pragmatic self-monitoring), and Audience-aware (one simulator per domain-specific listener, i.e. a ToM of a less-knowledgeable listener). Models are trained from scratch (no pretrained LLMs) to fully control knowledge asymmetry.",
      "datasets": "PhotoBook dataset (Haber et al. 2019) of task-oriented visually grounded English dialogues; specifically the referring-utterance extraction of Takmaz et al. 2020 (~41,340 utterances, each paired with a target image + 5 distractors; Table 1 lists 45,741 utterances after domain splitting, |V|=6,038, 324 images). Images are from MS-COCO across 30 visual domains, clustered into 5 macro-domains with minimal vocabulary overlap: appliances, food, indoor, outdoor, vehicles. Split 70/15/15 train/val/test; also an \"all-domains\" merge. ResNet-152 image features.",
      "baselines": "Baseline = speaker with no simulation/adaptation (egocentric generation). These two serve as the comparison points for the proposed audience-aware adaptation. Random-choice reference = 16.67% (1/6). Related-work baselines discussed but not directly compared: RSA framework, Greco et al. 2023 (RSA expert-to-layman), Singh et al. 2023 (listener subtraction).",
      "metrics": "Primary: listener resolution accuracy (communicative success), split into in-domain (IND) and out-of-domain (OOD); MRR for listeners. Simulator prediction accuracy. Analysis metrics: diagnostic probing accuracy (logistic regression predicting image-domain vs. listener-domain from h0 across adaptation steps), type-utterance ratio, type-token ratio, unigram POS distribution, Age-of-Acquisition (Kuperman et al. 2012), rate of lexical choice from target-image vs. listener-domain vocabulary.",
      "key_results": "Baseline: 52.30% IND / 19.06% OOD accuracy (OOD near random 16.67%). Self-aware adaptation: 65.09% IND but no OOD gain (19.11%). Probing shows adaptation replaces image-domain info in h0 with listener-domain info within ~3 gradient steps. Adapted vocabulary becomes more diverse (higher type-token ratio) but also more abstract (rising Age-of-Acquisition); successful adapted utterances use lower-AoA words, fewer target-image-domain words, and more listener-domain words.       "code_link": "https://github.com/nicofirst1/speaker-adaptation",
      "relevance": "Directly supports the audience-modeling hypothesis but via a decoding-time gradient mechanism rather than an LLM verbalizing audience simulation in chain-of-thought: it shows that explicitly predicting how the audience will react to a planned utterance, and revising accordingly, measurably improves communicative success (a concrete \"writing quality\" proxy = listener resolution). The self-aware vs. audience-aware contrast is a strong template for the experiment: modeling a specific audience (not a generic one) is what drives gains, arguing the intervention should condition on an actual reader model each sentence. Caveats for reuse: it is a constrained referring-game with a discriminative listener metric, uses trained-from-scratch LSTMs (not LLMs) and steers via backprop on hidden states rather than natural-language CoT; adaptation also risks disfluency/abstractness, echoing the \"annoying writing\" concern.       "title": "ToM-agent: Large Language Models as Theory of Mind Aware Generative Agents with Counterfactual Reflection",

      "key_contribution": "First to apply Theory-of-Mind (ToM) modeling to open-domain conversational generative agents by disentangling \"belief\" from \"confidence\" (rather than binary true/false-belief). Fully zero-shot (no training/annotation).",
      "methodology": "Two LLM-based agents converse: agent A generates utterances from its own (self-)BDIs; agent B tracks A's hidden BDIs. Three modules: (1) Self-BDI-aware module initializes A's BDIs zero-shot from a randomly sampled dialogue episode plus a \"reverse BDI argumentation\" step to push beliefs away from bland common sense; (2) Vanilla BDI tracking = infer top-1 BDI each turn and generate a reply (baseline); (3) CR-based tracking = infer top-k (k=3) BDIs with confidences summing to 100%, predict A's next utterance from the top-confidence BDI, score predicted-vs-real utterance similarity (0-1) via an embedding/similarity model, and run \"counterfactual reflection\" (what if the inferred BDI is wrong?) to produce an add/increase/decrease/delete/re-rank update plan over BDIs.       "baselines": "\"Without ToM\" (plain generation, no mental-state modeling); \"Vanilla ToM\" (top-1 BDI tracking, no reflection); \"Reflection-based ToM\" (ToM-agent without counterfactual component); vs the full \"CR-based ToM\" (counterfactual reflection).       "metrics": "First- and second-order ToM BDI inference scored as binary similarity (human-annotator score 0-5, avg, threshold 0.25 for similar/not-similar) → Precision, F1, Recall reported separately for Belief, Desire, Intention.       "key_results": "CR-based ToM achieves the best downstream results on both datasets and both models. Empathetic/GPT-4: AT 5.80 and SR@t 0.55 for CR-based vs 6.53/0.43 without ToM.       "methodology": "The task: given a conversation context and two equally valid but pragmatically different candidate replies, the evaluated model must generate a free-form explanation of the speaker's underlying intent and why that particular wording was chosen (not a yes/no or multiple-choice label). Evaluation uses LLM-as-a-judge (GPT-4.1) comparing model explanations against human-verified gold references, via (1) a 10-point scoring rubric per explanation and (2) pairwise win-rate between two model outputs, with the judge also classifying the winning explanation into cognitive-pragmatic / pragmalinguistic / sociopragmatic categories.       "baselines": "Base (pretrained) models serve as the reference baseline against which SFT and DPO stages are compared.       "title": "Reasoning about Pragmatics with Neural Listeners and Speakers",
      "year": "2016",
      "key_contribution": "A neural model that generates contrastive/pragmatic descriptions by combining a learned \"literal speaker\" (captioning model) and \"literal listener\" (retrieval model), then having a higher-order \"reasoning speaker\" (S1) explicitly select utterances that make the modeled listener behave correctly. It is the first learned captioning model to reason explicitly about listeners, and the first learned pragmatics approach requiring only ordinary (non-pragmatic) training data. This is a computational instantiation of Gricean/Rational-Speech-Acts audience design.",
      "methodology": "The task is a reference game: given a target referent r and a distractor r', produce a description that lets a listener pick the target. Base modules (referent encoder, description encoder, choice ranker, referent describer) are small feedforward nets trained on ordinary isolated captions. A literal listener L0 is trained contrastively (target vs. random distractor); a literal speaker S0 is a conditional neural language model. At inference, the reasoning speaker S1 samples n candidate descriptions from S0, then reranks each by pk = pS0(d|r)^lambda * pL0(target|d,r1,r2)^(1-lambda), selecting the argmax. The listener term rewards discriminativeness (audience will pick correctly); the speaker term regularizes toward fluent/human-like language, with lambda trading off fluency vs. informativeness (best at lambda=0.02, ~100 samples).",
      "datasets": "Abstract Scenes Dataset (Zitnick & Parikh 2013): 10,020 human-constructed clip-art scenes, each with up to 6 captions; scenes available as rendered images and as object/location feature vectors. Prior pragmatics work used the smaller TUNA corpus of isolated object descriptions or small synthetic datasets.",
      "baselines": "Literal (S0): a standard image-captioning model trained normally on captions, ignoring context (context-blind). Contrastive: a reimplementation of Mao et al. 2015's soft-contrastive referring-expression model. Compiled (S1): a \"reflex\" direct model trained on captions sampled from the reasoning speaker (distills reasoning into a feedforward decoder, no inference at test). Also a hand-engineered pragmatic feature baseline (indicators only on distinguishing objects).",
      "metrics": "Accuracy: human success rate on the reference game (AMT raters shown two images plus a model description, must pick the matching image). Fluency: human 1-5 Likert linguistic-quality rating of isolated sentences. Significance via Binomial test (p<0.05). Also reports accuracy vs. number of samples and accuracy broken down by number of scene differences.",
      "key_results": "Reasoning speaker S1 reached 81% test accuracy (All) and 68% (Hard), vs. Contrastive 69%/58% and Literal 64%/53% — a 17% absolute gain on All and 15% on Hard over prior work; all differences significant. Dev accuracy: S1 83/73, Contrastive 71/54, Literal 66/54. Adding a tiny speaker weight (lambda=0.02) gave near-perfect fluency (4.8/5, matching the pure literal speaker) with almost no accuracy loss. Accuracy rose with samples (66% at 1 -> 83% at 100, plateauing). The \"compiled\" reflex model failed to recover reasoning gains (mean 69% vs 83%), indicating explicit inference-time reasoning about the listener is not easily distilled into a shallow feedforward net.",
      "code_link": "http://github.com/jacobandreas/pragma (models, human annotations, and code to regenerate all tables/figures)",
      "relevance": "Strong conceptual support for the hypothesis: performance improves substantially when the speaker explicitly simulates an audience/listener model and selects utterances that make that audience respond correctly, rather than generating context-blind (annoying/generic) text. Crucially, the audience-design signal is applied per-utterance via a rerank-by-simulated-listener step — directly analogous to having an LLM simulate the audience in its chain-of-thought before committing each sentence. The lambda trade-off is a concrete precedent for the fluency-vs-audience-informativeness tension a ToM intervention must balance. Reusable for an LLM experiment: the reference-game task, human accuracy + fluency metrics, and the context-blind captioner as a baseline map cleanly onto \"with vs. without simulated-audience CoT\" — though it uses tiny task-specific nets and a discriminative-listener rerank, not free-form LLM self-simulation, so the audience is a separate trained model rather than the generator reasoning about a reader in-context."

      "methodology": "1) Sample n=10 code candidates for an instruction I0 (temperature 1.2, Python AST filter for validity). 2) For each candidate c_i, use the same base model (greedy decoding) to reverse-generate a candidate-induced instruction I_i describing what c_i does, forming an induced instruction set {I0,...,In}. 3) Compute literal-compatibility scores L0(I_j, c_i)=log P(c_i | I_j) for every instruction-candidate pair (raw sequence log-likelihood over code tokens). 4) Build local pairwise pragmatic contests: margin Δ_ij = [L0(I0,c_i)-L0(I_j,c_i)] - [L0(I0,c_j)-L0(I_i,c_j)], aggregated into a tournament-style win-rate (normalized Copeland score) \"pair_i\". 5) Add a global-support term avg_i = mean over instructions of log P(c_i | I_j) as a prior-like stabilizer. 6) Final score = z(pair_i)+z(avg_i) (within-task standardized, equal weight), pick argmax.
