      "key_contribution": "A critique/position paper showing that GPT-3.5's apparent success on classic Theory-of-Mind (ToM) false-belief tasks (as reported by Kosinski 2023) is brittle: small, ToM-preserving perturbations of the vignettes flip the model's belief attributions to wrong answers. It argues model evaluation for intuitive psychology should adopt a skeptical zero-hypothesis in which outlying failures outweigh average success rates.",
      "methodology": "Using the exact same setup as Kosinski (posing a text vignette to GPT-3.5 and reading off the probabilities of alternative next-word completions for \"content\" and \"belief\" prompts), the author manually authors targeted perturbations of two classic false-belief tasks: Unexpected Contents (the \"Smarties\"/popcorn-vs-chocolate bag) and Unexpected Transfer (Sally-Anne, John/Mark/cat). Each perturbation preserves ToM logic but should change the correct belief attribution: making the container transparent (perceptual access), making the label uninformative (Sam cannot read), adding trusted testimony that contradicts the label, moving when the label is read to the end / having the protagonist fill and label the bag themselves, changing spatial relation \"in\" to \"on,\" and querying the mental state of the mover (Mark) rather than the naive agent. For each variant the model's completion probabilities (e.g., P(popcorn) vs P(chocolate)) are recorded to show the model still gives the \"false-belief\" answer even when it is now incorrect. No fine-tuning, no aggregate benchmark, no statistical testing; the analysis is a hand-crafted adversarial probe set.",
      "datasets": "No released dataset. Uses ~8 hand-written vignette variants derived from the two tasks in Kosinski (2023): Unexpected Contents variations 1A Transparent Access, 1B Uninformative Label (cannot read), 1C Trusted/Trustworthy Testimony, 1D Late Labels; Unexpected Transfer variations 2A Transparent Access, 2B Relationship Change (in->on), 2C Trusted Communication, 2D Querying the Other Person (Mark). Original Kosinski materials/methods are noted as publicly available. Illustrative figures generated with DALL-E 2 but images were not evaluated.",
      "baselines": "The implicit baseline is Kosinski (2023) \"Theory of Mind May Have Spontaneously Emerged in LLMs,\" reporting near-100% GPT-3.5 accuracy on the unperturbed tasks. Related pessimistic prior work: Sap et al. 2022 (Neural ToM limits). No quantitative baseline comparison is run; the paper contrasts unperturbed vs perturbed prompt completions on the same GPT-3.5 model.",
      "metrics": "Next-token/completion probabilities assigned by GPT-3.5 to the two competing continuations (e.g., P(popcorn) vs P(chocolate); P(box) vs P(basket)/P(chest)) for a \"content prompt\" and two \"belief prompts\" (\"She/He believes the bag/cat is...\" and a second desire-framed prompt \"She is delighted... loves eating...\"). Correctness is judged qualitatively as whether the model's high-probability completion matches the ToM-correct answer.",
      "key_results": "Every ToM-preserving perturbation flipped GPT-3.5 to the wrong belief attribution. Unexpected Contents: 1A transparent P(chocolate)=95%, 1B cannot-read P(chocolate)=98%, 1C trusted testimony P(chocolate)=97%, 1D self-filled/self-labeled P(chocolate)=87% — all should be \"popcorn.\" Unexpected Transfer: 2A transparent chest P(chest)=94%, 2B in->on P(basket)=97%, 2C trusted communication P(basket)=97%, 2D asking about the mover Mark P(basket)=99% (Mark himself moved the cat, so correct answer is box). The model was also shown sensitive to spurious cues (e.g., a double space changing the answer). Conclusion: ToM has probably not spontaneously emerged; the author advocates integrating structured cognitive ToM models with LLMs and warns that publishing a systematic variation generator/benchmark would let LLMs \"gobble up\" data to pass without genuinely learning ToM.",
      "code_link": "none found (author notes Kosinski 2023's materials are public but provides no repository for this paper)",
      "relevance": "Only indirectly relevant to the audience-simulation-in-chain-of-thought hypothesis. It provides no writing task, no CoT intervention, and no evidence that prompting a model to simulate another mind improves output — in fact it shows the opposite failure mode: default GPT-3.5 does NOT robustly model another agent's belief state, which motivates why an explicit audience-modeling step might help. It offers reusable false-belief probe templates and the completion-probability evaluation paradigm that could be adapted to test whether an explicit \"simulate the reader\" CoT step improves ToM-sensitive reasoning, but it is a diagnostic/critique paper, not an intervention or writing-quality study."
    },
    {
      "file": "2305.14763_clever_hans_or_neural_tom_stress_testing.pdf",
      "title": "Clever Hans or Neural Theory of Mind? Stress Testing Social Reasoning in Large Language Models",
      "year": "2023",
      "key_contribution": "A stress-test of whether LLMs possess robust Neural Theory-of-Mind (N-ToM), spanning 6 ToM tasks and 15 LLMs in zero-shot. Introduces two new evaluation resources (Adv-CSFB, an adversarial commonsense false-belief set built on Kosinski/Ullman, and ToMi', a sentence-completion adaptation of ToMi) and shows LLM ToM performance is brittle, dropping sharply on adversarial variants, evidence of shallow heuristics rather than genuine ToM.",
      "methodology": "The authors evaluate 15 LLMs (FlanT5 family, FlanUL2, GPT-3 davinci-002/003, GPT-3.5-turbo, GPT-4, Jurassic-2 family) zero-shot across 6 ToM datasets, comparing to a most-frequent-class (MFC) baseline. They compare three probing methods: LM-probing (pick highest-probability option), MC-probing (present context+question+choices, model emits the letter), and CoT-probing (reason step-by-step then answer). To test robustness they build Adv-CSFB by taking Kosinski's false-belief items and adding true-belief controls plus adversarial perturbations from Ullman (transparent access, uninformative label, trusted testimony, late labels, in-to-on, other person), then measure accuracy per adversarial category on belief-focused questions. They also stratify existing datasets (ToMi by question type, SocialIQa by main-character vs. others) to expose difficulty gaps hidden by aggregate scores.",
      "datasets": "TriangleCOPA (100 items); SocialIQa (400 sampled from 37,588); ToMi (400 sampled from 1000+ synthetic false-belief stories, memory/reality/first-&second-order); ToMi' (this paper: 30 stories x 6 = 180 questions, ToMi converted to sentence-completion format); Epistemic Reasoning from BIG-bench (2000 NLI items with factive/non-factive verbs); Adv-CSFB (this paper: 183 questions / 40 stories; 110 unexpected-contents + 73 unexpected-transfer, split into false-belief, true-belief, adversarial); FauxPas-EAI (176 questions / 44 stories, 22 faux-pas + 22 control, 4 questions each). Code and data at github.com/salavi/Clever_Hans_or_N-ToM.",
      "baselines": "Most-Frequent-Class (MFC) baseline per dataset (the primary sanity baseline). Cross-model comparison across 15 LLMs of varying size/family. Comparison across probing methods (LM-, MC-, CoT-probing) treated as internal baselines against each other. Comparison of ToMi' vs. ToM-k (Kosinski) to test spurious correlations; replication of Kosinski (2023) and Ullman (2023) prior results.",
      "metrics": "Accuracy (per-dataset, per-question-type, per-adversarial-category). Question-level accuracy and story-level \"joint score\" (all questions in a story must be correct) for FauxPas-EAI, Adv-CSFB, and ToMi'. Average-across-splits and lowest-split-score recommended as a robustness/difficulty metric. Entailment accuracy for Epistemic Reasoning. T-test used for the data-contamination analysis on SocialIQa standard vs. secret test set.",
      "key_results": "No model robustly exhibits ToM across all 6 datasets. Some datasets are near-solved (TriangleCOPA 96% flan-t5-xxl; GPT-4 97% on original false-belief items) while others fail badly (GPT-4 only 27% story-level on FauxPas-EAI, below MFC). On Adv-CSFB, adversarial perturbations collapse performance: e.g., GPT-4 drops to 0% on transparent-access and in-to-on transfer while scoring 97.5% on plain false belief. ToMi' scores far below ToM-k (GPT-4: 20% all / 52% no-second-order vs. 87% on ToM-k), indicating reliance on Sally-Anne pattern matching. Probing method matters (CoT vs. MC differ up to 7% on SocialIQa). Stratified splits reveal hidden weaknesses (GPT-3.5 drops from 81% to 46% on ToMi false-belief subset; GPT-4 from 79% to 74.5% on SocialIQa \"others\"). Newer instruction-tuned models often do WORSE on adversarial items, attributed to reporting bias and over-cooperativeness (treating every mentioned detail as relevant).",
      "code_link": "https://github.com/salavi/Clever_Hans_or_N-ToM",
      "relevance": "Only indirectly relevant. This paper is about whether LLMs can *answer questions about* others' mental states, not about whether prompting a model to *simulate the audience while writing* improves writing quality. It does not test audience-design or generation quality; its tasks are multiple-choice/completion comprehension. However, it is highly useful as cautionary methodology for any ToM-intervention experiment: (1) its finding that CoT can \"excessively guide\" models by leaking task structure warns that a per-sentence audience-simulation CoT could inflate results without genuine modeling; (2) its adversarial/true-belief control design and joint-score metric offer a template for verifying an intervention gives real gains rather than shortcut gains; (3) it shows LLMs' modeling of others' beliefs is brittle, which motivates but also tempers the hypothesis that models spontaneously model an audience."
    },
    {
      "file": "2502.06470_survey_theory_of_mind_in_llms.pdf",
      "title": "A Survey of Theory of Mind in Large Language Models: Evaluations, Representations, and Safety Risks",
      "year": "2025",
      "key_contribution": "A short survey/position paper (AAAI 2025, Apart Research) that synthesizes the literature on Theory of Mind (ToM) in LLMs. Its three claims are: (1) LLMs can match human performance on some specific ToM tasks but ToM remains limited and non-robust; (2) LLMs contain internal, linearly-decodable representations of self/other belief states that causally affect ToM performance; (3) advanced ToM introduces safety risks (privacy inference, deception, anthropomorphism, multi-agent collusion/escalation). It also recommends future evaluation directions beyond QA benchmarks toward authentic deployment settings.",
      "methodology": "This is a literature survey with no new algorithm, model, or experiment. It organizes prior work into behavioural ToM evaluation (QA benchmarks and psychological tests), representational/interpretability ToM (linear probes on residual-stream activations that extract belief states, and activation steering that causally changes false-belief answers), sources of future capability gains (scaling, prompting such as perspective-taking, test-time compute, scaffolding), and a taxonomy of safety risks split into user-facing (privacy/social engineering, deceptive behaviour, unintentional anthropomorphism) and multi-agent (exploitation, conflict escalation, collective misalignment). Mitigation strategies discussed include model unlearning, activation/representation engineering, and latent adversarial training. Note: the survey frames ToM as belief attribution and social reasoning, not as writing/audience-design.",
      "datasets": "No new dataset. It cites (does not run) ToM benchmarks reusable elsewhere: BigToM (Gandhi et al. 2023), FANToM (Kim et al. 2023, interaction stress-test), OpenToM (Xu et al. 2024), Hi-ToM (Wu et al. 2023, higher-order ToM), ToMBench (Chen et al. 2024b, ACL). Also references human-comparison test sets from van Duijn et al. 2023 (children aged 7-10), Strachan et al. 2024 (Nature Human Behaviour), and adversarial/stress sets from Shapira et al. 2024 and Ullman 2023. No sizes reported.",
      "baselines": "None run by the paper. Models discussed in cited works include GPT-4 and (via probing work) Pythia-70m and other open models. Perspective-taking prompting (Wilf et al. 2024, \"Think Twice\") is cited as a prompting intervention that improves ToM.",
      "metrics": "No metrics computed. Cited results use QA accuracy on ToM benchmarks (e.g., false-belief, irony detection, k-th order ToM accuracy), linear-probe decoding accuracy for belief states (near-perfect internal probing; ~80% transfer to real human conversations in Chen et al. 2024a), and human-vs-LLM comparison scores.",
      "key_results": "Qualitative/survey findings: LLMs (GPT-4) reach 7-10 year-old-child to adult-human level on some standard ToM tasks and can outperform humans on 6th-order ToM (Street et al. 2024), yet fail on hard/adversarial benchmarks (BigToM, FANToM, OpenToM, Hi-ToM, ToMBench) and trivial task alterations, indicating non-robust ToM. Linear probes recover others' belief states and steering with them significantly changes false-belief answers; probing accuracy rises with model size and fine-tuning. The paper argues current ToM QA benchmarks suffer from contamination/overfitting and calls for evaluation in authentic deployment scenarios (personal assistants, multi-agent environments, simulated social platforms).",
      "code_link": "none found (survey paper; no released code or data)",
      "relevance": "Only tangentially relevant. The paper supports the premise's foundation-that LLMs have (limited, non-robust) capacity to model others' mental states and can be prompted into better perspective-taking (Wilf et al. 2024, \"Think Twice\")-but it never studies writing quality or audience design, and offers no dataset, metric, or baseline for judging whether per-sentence audience simulation in chain-of-thought improves text. For the hypothesis, its concrete reuse is limited to pointers: the cited perspective-taking prompting method as a prior for the intervention, and its critique that ToM should be tested in realistic generation/deployment settings rather than QA. To test the writing hypothesis one would still need external writing-quality benchmarks and human/LLM-judge evaluations not provided here."
    },
    {
      "file": "2407.06004_perceptions_to_beliefs_precursory_inferences_tom.pdf",
      "title": "Perceptions to Beliefs: Exploring Precursory Inferences for Theory of Mind in Large Language Models",
      "year": "2024",
      "key_contribution": "Decomposes LLM theory-of-mind into two developmental precursors: perception inference (who saw/heard each piece of info) and perception-to-belief inference (deriving beliefs from those perceptions). Introduces two perception-annotated benchmarks (Percept-ToMi, Percept-FANToM) and a training-free framework, PercepToM, that has the LLM infer per-sentence/per-utterance perceivers, string-match to extract only the target character's perceived context, then answer the ToM question on that isolated context.",
      "methodology": "The core intervention (PercepToM) is a 3-step pipeline: (1) the LLM is prompted to output a JSON array mapping each sentence/utterance in the context to the set of characters who perceived it (perception inference); (2) a deterministic string-matching step extracts the \"perspective context\" — the subset of the input actually perceived by the target character; (3) the LLM answers the ToM question conditioned only on that filtered perspective context. This converts a false-belief scenario into an information-symmetric true-belief scenario. A key diagnostic finding: even when handed ground-truth perception annotations, LLMs still fail at belief inference because they cannot suppress irrelevant (non-perceived) information — a deficit the authors term weak \"inhibitory control.\" Explicitly separating perception tracking from the answer, and physically removing unperceived context, is what drives gains.",
      "datasets": "Percept-ToMi (extends Fixed & Disambiguated ToMi; 300 true-belief + 300 false-belief contexts, ~1802/1804 questions each, perceivers auto-annotated via SymbolicToM then hand-corrected) and Percept-FANToM (extends FANToM multi-party conversations; 340 true-belief + 539 false-belief contexts, 13161/25279 questions, perceivers manually annotated by join/leave times). Source: ToMi (first/second-order true/false belief QA) and FANToM (multi-party dialogue). Released CC BY-NC 4.0.",
      "baselines": "Vanilla (direct answer), Chain-of-Thought (\"Let's think step by step\"), System 2 Attention (S2A, LLM extracts relevant context first), SymbolicToM (graph-based belief tracker, ToMi only), and SimToM (perspective-taking prompt). PercepToM and a PercepToM+Oracle (ground-truth perceptions) ablation.",
      "metrics": "ToMi: accuracy = ratio of correctly answered story-question pairs. FANToM: set:ALL score (must answer all 5 ToM question types correctly for the same info). Perception inference accuracy = ratio of info units with correctly identified perceivers, averaged over contexts. Pearson correlation between ToM and precursor performance.",
      "key_results": "LLMs are strong at perception inference (avg acc 0.781 ToMi, 0.926 FANToM) but weak at perception-to-belief inference, especially false-belief. PercepToM beats Vanilla/CoT/S2A in most settings: GPT-4 Turbo reaches 1.000 on ToMi false-belief; GPT-4o rises from ~0.017 (vanilla) to 0.566 on FANToM false-belief; Llama-3-70B from ~0 to 0.147. Ablation: PercepToM+Oracle >> plain perception-to-belief, showing removing irrelevant info (inhibitory control) is the key lever. Perception inference accuracy correlates weakly/none with ToM; perception-to-belief correlates positively (r up to ~0.68).",
      "code_link": "No explicit GitHub URL in the paper; authors state benchmarks will be released publicly (CC BY-NC 4.0). arXiv:2407.06004. Code link: none found in text.",
      "relevance": "Moderately relevant. The paper's central mechanism — prompting the LLM to explicitly model, per sentence/utterance, whose perspective perceives each piece of content, then conditioning the answer on that mental-state model — is a direct instantiation of \"simulate the audience in the chain-of-thought each sentence\" for a comprehension task. It provides a concrete per-unit ToM-annotation format, prompts (Appendix C/D), and the finding that explicit audience/perspective modeling improves output, plus baselines (CoT, S2A, SimToM) reusable as comparison conditions. Caveat: it targets reading-comprehension QA, not open-ended writing quality, so it supports the plausibility of the intervention but does not test writing-quality outcomes directly."
    },
    {
      "file": "2504.07532_ai_slop_to_ai_polish_edit_based_writing_rewards.pdf",
      "title": "AI-Slop to AI-Polish? Aligning Language Models through Edit-Based Writing Rewards and Test-time Computation",
      "year": "2025 (COLM 2025; arXiv 2504.07532v3, Aug 2025)",
      "key_contribution": "Introduces (1) the Writing Quality Benchmark (WQ), consolidating five writing-preference datasets into 4,729 pairwise judgments where SOTA LLMs barely beat chance; (2) Writing Quality Reward Models (WQRM) trained on implicit preferences from expert edits (the LAMP corpus) that reach 74% accuracy and beat GPT-4o/o1/DeepSeek-R1/Skywork; and (3) an edit-based, three-step editing pipeline plus best-of-N test-time selection validated by 9 professional writers.",
      "methodology": "The core signal is implicit preference from expert edits: the LAMP corpus (AI-generated paragraph + MFA-expert-edited version, with 1-10 quality scores) is transformed into pairwise (P), scalar-regression (R), and combined (PR) training points. Reward models are trained in two families: ModernBERT-Large encoders (regression head with scaled sigmoid to 1-10, aparametric cosine-similarity classification head) and generative Llama3.1-70B via QLoRA outputting JSON preferences/scores. Generative variants are augmented with LLM-generated (Claude 3.5) \"silver rationales\" (contrastive for P, absolute critique for R, citing excerpts), used either on input (IR->O) or as output (I->RO); rationales are training-only, not available at test time. A separate three-step CoT editing model (SFT of GPT-4o/Llama3.1-70B on LAMP edit traces) detects idiosyncratic spans, rewrites each as an executable string-replace edit, and executes them; best-of-N=20 candidates are scored by WQRM and the top one selected.",
      "datasets": "LAMP (Language model Authored, Manually Polished): 1,282 <AI-generated, expert-edited> paragraph pairs (150-400 words, fiction/non-fiction), ~8 edits/passage, split 1,000 train / 67 val / 215 test; transformed into 2,000 (P), 2,000 (R), 4,000 (PR) training points. WQ Benchmark (4,729 pairs total) = Art or Artifice (144, flash fiction 1.5-3k words, human+GPT-3.5/GPT-4/Claude), LAMP-test (1,206), Style Mimic (300; MFA-student vs award-winning-author, Human-Human), Synthetic Mirror (1,120; author vs GPT-4o/Claude-3.5 mirror), LM Arena (1,959; crowd-sourced creative-writing pairs). Validation: 100 drafts (50 GPT-4o, 50 Llama3.1-70B) over 90 instructions across fiction/non-fiction/marketing; 300 expert triplet rankings. Content experiment: 50 Style Mimic instructions in 30-word vs ~180-word variants. Extra calibration data: 100 award-author paragraphs (score 10) + 80 MFA-published paragraphs (score 7.5).",
      "baselines": "Zero-shot/few-shot LLMs: GPT-4o, GPT-4o (5-shot), O1, O3, DeepSeek-R1; generic reward model Skywork-Reward-8B-v0.2 (top on RewardBench); Pangram AI-detector; and their own Llama3.1-70B WQRM variants vs ModernBERT WQRM variants. Editing-pipeline validation baselines: First Draft, Random Edit, Best Edit (WQRM-selected). Writing-quality profiling across GPT-4.5/4o/4o-mini/3.5, Claude 3.7/3.5/3-Haiku, Gemini 2.5-Pro/2.0-Flash/1.5-Pro/1.5-Flash, vs award-winning authors and MFA students.",
      "metrics": "Pairwise preference accuracy on WQ (per-dataset + overall; random = 50%); reward-model calibration (WQRM score gap vs expert-agreement %); sensitivity (WQRM score change per applied edit); expert human evaluation via triplet ranking (average rank, top-rank %, and overall/high-gap agreement between WQRM Best Edit and expert majority).",
      "key_results": "Zero-shot LLMs, including reasoning models (O1, R1), performed near random on WQ (GPT-4o 40.9%, O1 51.7%, R1 46.0%); out-of-the-box CoT reasoning did NOT help writing-quality assessment. GPT-4o few-shot rose 40.9->54.3 but no further gains. Best WQRM (MBERT-WQRM-PR) hit 74.3% overall (beating baselines ~10%+). Rationale augmentation (IR->O) improved generative models. WQRM is well-calibrated (agreement rises 55%->80% as score gap grows) and sensitive (+0.2 median score per edit). Best Edit ranked first overall (avg rank 1.58 vs 2.09 random-edit, 2.26 first-draft); experts agreed with WQRM 66% overall, 72.2% when gap>1. Even with detailed content, LLMs (~7.0 median) trailed MFA students (8.6) and award authors (8.9).",
      "code_link": "https://github.com/salesforce/creativity_eval (datasets and models released)",
      "relevance": "Directly relevant as a rigorous testbed and negative-baseline for the ToM/audience-design hypothesis: the paper shows generic chain-of-thought reasoning (O1/R1/O3) does NOT improve writing-quality assessment, and that per-sentence-style span-level self-editing (detect/rewrite/execute) plus best-of-N does help — offering a concrete comparison point for whether audience-simulation CoT beats plain CoT. Its WQRM reward model, WQ benchmark, LAMP edit corpus, editing pipeline, and 3-domain expert-ranking protocol are all reusable to score whether an audience-simulating writer produces higher-quality text. However, its notion of quality is intrinsic-craft (idiosyncrasies/cliches), not explicit reader-modeling, so audience-design would be a novel intervention layered on this evaluation scaffold rather than something the paper itself tests."

content.content:
148	    },
149	    {
150	      "file": "2601.06116_homogenization_problem_in_llms_diversity.pdf",
151	      "title": "The Homogenization Problem in LLMs: Towards Meaningful Diversity in AI Safety",
152	      "year": "2026",
153	      "key_contribution": "A conceptual/formal framework (grounded in queer theory) for characterizing \"homogenization\" — the collapse of LLM generations toward normative \"default\" modes — by modeling an LLM as a probability distribution over a tree of strings and scoring outputs against user-defined \"structures.\" Introduces \"xeno-reproduction\" as a class of diversity-promoting interventions and demonstrates the framework with a gender-bias case study on Claude 3.5 Haiku generating nurse love stories.",
154	      "methodology": "Formalizes an LLM as a distribution over a trie of token trajectories; each prefix indexes a subtree with its own distribution. Analysts define \"structures\" (scoring functions alpha: string -> [0,1]) that encode axes of difference; a \"system\" is a vector of structures, and the \"system barycenter\" (structure default) is the expected structure score over continuations from a prefix, estimated by Monte Carlo sampling of K continuations and scored by an LLM-as-judge ensemble. \"Deviance\"/\"orientation\" measure a trajectory's signed deviation from this default. Homogenization is formalized as barycenter entropy -> 0 plus expected-deviance and deviance-variance -> 0; xeno-reproduction reweights sampling (trajectory-level: P(y|x,w) ∝ P_base * exp(beta * deviance(y|x))) to push outputs away from the default subject to constraints. The case study branches a shared story trunk on gendered pronoun prefills to trace how the barycenter shifts.",
155	      "datasets": "No external benchmark dataset. Self-generated case-study corpus: 5 \"arms\" (root, trunk, branch_1 \"his partner\", branch_2 \"her partner\", branch_3 \"their partner\") x 201 trajectories each (200 sampled at temp=1.0 + 1 greedy) = ~1005 nurse love-story generations from Claude 3.5 Haiku (claude-3-5-haiku-20241022), max 512 tokens. Real-world reference proportions drawn from BLS CPS 2023, NCSBN 2024 nursing survey, Sabin et al. 2015, Gallup 2024.",
156	      "baselines": "No model/algorithm baselines in the empirical sense. The \"baseline\" is the unintervened LLM distribution (the root/unmarked prompt barycenter) compared against gender-marked branches and against a real-world statistical reference distribution. Appendix G maps the framework onto existing linguistic-diversity metrics (lexical n-gram, syntactic POS, semantic embedding, Vendi Score, MAUVE) as expressible special cases rather than experimental comparisons.",
157	      "metrics": "System barycenter (per-structure expected score, 4 structures: alpha_male, alpha_female, alpha_hetero, alpha_same-sex), expected deviance E[partial_n] (dimension-normalized L2 / RMS orientation), deviance variance Var[partial_n], barycenter entropy H (and effective number of structures exp(H) / Hill numbers), forking magnitude Delta_k, and a branch-probability estimator. Inter-judge agreement/calibration also reported.",
158	      "key_results": "Unprompted, Claude defaults to a female nurse (alpha_female≈0.73) in a heterosexual couple (alpha_hetero≈0.99), almost always named Sarah paired with Marcus; male and same-sex scores near zero. Marking \"her partner\" (branch_2) drives deviance to 0.035 (below root 0.319) — the most homogenizing arm. Only \"his partner\" (branch_1) substantially disrupts the default (deviance 0.622; male 0.867), and it triggers an implicit same-sex association (same-sex rises to 0.199) despite no same-sex prefill. Notably, the female-nurse bias is \"stronger than markedness\": ~9/200 branch_1 trajectories re-cast the nurse as female despite explicit \"his.\" Three-judge ensemble (Claude Opus, GPT-5, Gemini 2.5 Flash) agreed qualitatively but differed in calibration.",
159	      "code_link": "none found (no repository URL in paper; Appendix acknowledges Claude Code was used for the pipeline)",
160	      "relevance": "Only indirectly relevant. The paper is about diversity/bias homogenization, not audience-modeling, and it contains no ToM/audience-simulation intervention, so it does not test the hypothesis. However, it offers directly reusable experimental machinery for a writing-quality study: (1) an LLM-as-judge ensemble with a chain-of-thought scoring scaffold (enumerate characters/markers -> decide -> \"ANSWER: 0/1\"), three-judge soft scoring, and regex verdict parsing — a validated template for scoring generated writing along audience-relevant \"structures\"; (2) the tree/prefix \"forking token\" and per-sentence barycenter-tracking method (Appendix H measures the system default position-by-position along a generation), which is a concrete way to instrument writing sentence-by-sentence, matching the hypothesis's \"every sentence or so\" granularity; and (3) a homogenization/diversity metric (deviance, barycenter entropy, NoveltyBench-adjacent measures cited) that could quantify whether an audience-simulation prompt reduces annoying sameness. The nurse love-story generation setup and the semantic-similarity \"writing style from references\" structure (Appendix B.5, e.g. Gen-Z vs Boomer register) are reusable for building an audience-design writing task and evaluation."
161	    },
162	    {
163	      "file": "2211.08371_pragmatics_in_language_grounding_survey.pdf",
164	      "title": "Pragmatics in Language Grounding: Phenomena, Tasks, and Modeling Approaches",
165	      "year": "2023 (arXiv v3, Nov 2023; originally 2022)",
166	      "key_contribution": "A survey/position paper by Fried, Tomlin, Hu, Patel, and Nematzadeh arguing that LLMs lack direct access to the contexts that drive pragmatic (audience-sensitive) meaning and therefore fail at context-dependent communication. It organizes grounded language tasks, pragmatic phenomena, and computational modeling frameworks (notably Rational Speech Acts) into a coherent taxonomy and offers recommendations for designing tasks and evaluations that elicit and reward audience-design/theory-of-mind behavior.",
167	      "methodology": "This is a literature survey with no new algorithm or experiment. It taxonomizes (a) types of context that enrich meaning (norms of interaction, goals/joint actions, common knowledge, discourse context, visual, embodied); (b) roles of pragmatics (reasoning about alternatives, ambiguity, collaborative planning, convention formation, mutual exclusivity/efficiency); (c) grounded tasks along a complexity spectrum (reference games, image captioning, instruction following, grounded dialogue) characterized by partial observability, symmetry, and iterated interaction; and (d) modeling frameworks. The central modeling framework is Rational Speech Acts (RSA), a recursive process where a rational speaker chooses utterances using an embedded model of how a listener will interpret them, and the listener reasons counterfactually about why the speaker chose that utterance over alternatives — a formalization of audience-modeling / theory-of-mind that directly parallels the hypothesis. It also covers multi-turn/POMDP-style planning approaches and Gricean maxims.",
168	      "datasets": "Survey references existing datasets rather than introducing one. Grounded (Table 1): ReferItGame (Kazemzadeh 2014), Colors in Context (Monroe 2017), Abstract Scenes (Andreas & Klein 2016), Conceptual Captions (Sharma 2018), SHRDLURN (Wang 2016), CerealBar (Suhr 2019a), Hexagons (Lachmy 2022), Cards Corpus (Potts 2012), OneCommon (Udagawa & Aizawa 2019), PhotoBook (Haber 2019). Unimodal text pragmatics (Appendix B, most relevant to a text-writing experiment): IMPPRES (implicature/presupposition), NOPE (presuppositions), Schuster 2020 and Li 2021 (human scalar-implicature rates), SARC (sarcasm). No sizes given in the paper.",
169	      "baselines": "No experiments, so no baselines run. As conceptual baselines/comparison points it discusses: general-purpose (multimodal) LLMs conditioning only on text (GPT-2/GPT, BART, CLIP, Flamingo) versus explicit pragmatic models built on top of a literal base model. Concrete instantiations cited: RSA-augmented neural speakers/listeners (Monroe 2017; Andreas & Klein 2016; Cohn-Gordon 2018), pragmatic instruction generation/following (Fried 2018a,b), Liu 2023 (RSA + meta-learning with GPT in a reference game), FAIR 2022 (BART + planning in Diplomacy), Kojima 2021 (GPT-2 fine-tuned to adapt to human followers).",
170	      "metrics": "Recommends evaluating on two axes beyond corpus overlap: (1) task success — accuracy of a listener selecting the intended referent/carrying out the action; measured via communication-based evaluation / self-play, where a speaker's generation is fed to a separate listener model (Newman 2020), with a strong caveat to make the listener model dissimilar from the system and to use human evaluation when possible. (2) communicative efficiency/cost — time to task completion, utterance length and complexity (Effenberger 2021), lexical entrainment/adaptation over repeated interaction (Hawkins 2020), and human quality ratings (Kojima 2021). It explicitly criticizes classical reference-based NLG metrics (BLEU-style) as unable to measure whether generated language will be understood correctly.",
171	      "key_results": "No quantitative results (survey). Key qualitative claims: LLMs match humans on some pragmatic tasks but fail on social-expectation violations and theory-of-mind (cites Sap 2022, Hu 2022, Ruis 2022); multimodal LLMs fail at word-to-concept mapping and visio-linguistic composition. Explicit audience modeling via RSA has empirically improved reference games, instruction following/generation, captioning, summarization, MT, and dialogue. Stated limitations of RSA relevant to the hypothesis: it assumes a fixed known set of candidate utterances and, except for a few cases, is limited to single-turn interactions; multi-turn variants typically drop higher-order theory-of-mind to stay tractable.",
172	      "code_link": "none found (no artifact released; it is a survey. Individual surveyed systems e.g. Colors-in-Context, PhotoBook, CerealBar have their own public repos)",
173	      "relevance": "Strongly conceptually relevant, weakly empirically. The paper's core thesis is essentially the hypothesis: LLMs write/communicate poorly because they do not model the audience/context, and explicit audience-design (RSA-style: pick each utterance by simulating how the listener interprets it vs. alternatives) improves communication — reported to help across generation tasks. It supplies the theoretical vocabulary (audience design, common ground, RSA, communication-based/self-play evaluation, efficiency metrics) and directly usable evaluation designs: feed a model's sentence to a separate 'listener/audience' model and score comprehension + efficiency, using a dissimilar audience model, plus human ratings. However, it provides no ready-made text-writing dataset, no chain-of-thought/per-sentence simulation protocol, and its tasks are grounded reference/dialogue games rather than open-ended prose; the unimodal datasets (IMPPRES, NOPE, SARC) and the communication-based evaluation setup are the most directly borrowable pieces for an experiment testing per-sentence audience simulation."
174	    },
175	    {
176	      "file": "2507.14063_collaborative_rational_speech_act_multiturn_dialog.pdf",
177	      "title": "Collaborative Rational Speech Act: Pragmatic Reasoning for Multi-Turn Dialog",
178	      "year": "2025 (arXiv 2507.14063v2, Sep 2025)",
179	      "key_contribution": "Introduces Collaborative Rational Speech Act (CRSA), an information-theoretic extension of the Rational Speech Act framework that models multi-turn collaborative dialog by maximizing a per-turn gain function generalized from rate-distortion theory. Unlike prior RSA, CRSA jointly models each agent's belief about (i) the shared task target and (ii) the interlocutor's private (unobserved) information, conditioning current utterances explicitly on dialog history.",
180	      "methodology": "CRSA extends RSA to a setting where two agents each hold a private meaning space (M_A, M_B) and share a task target space Y. It defines per-turn speaker S_t(u_t | m_St, w_t) and listener L_t(y | m_Lt, u_t, w_t) distributions conditioned on dialog history w_t, derived via alternating maximization of a gain = conditional entropy H(U_t|M_St,W_t) + alpha * expected listener value. A key term B_{S,t}(m_Lt) is interpreted as the speaker's belief over the listener's private meaning, updated from past utterances. Agents alternate speaker/listener roles each turn. Two initialization modes: a hand-specified lexicon (reference game) or an LLM literal speaker S^0_t(u_t|m_St,w_t) proportional to P_LM(u_t | w_t, prompt(m_St)) using LLaMA-3.2-1B-Instruct (medical dialog). Note: the model requires a predefined finite set of possible utterances per turn and computes probabilities over that fixed set, not open-ended generation.",
181	      "datasets": "Two settings. (1) Synthetic reference game adapted from Khani et al. 2018: N cards each with a letter (A/B, seen only by Agent A) and number (1/2, seen only by Agent B); agents collaboratively identify position of the \"A1\" card; evaluated over 500 rounds. (2) MDDial (Macherla et al. 2023): template-based doctor-patient differential-diagnosis dialogs; patient assigned a subset of predefined symptoms, doctor must identify correct disease from a set of pathologies; experiments use the train split of 1878 samples.",
182	      "baselines": "Reference game: YRSA (private-meaning RSA without multi-turn conditioning), YRSA-Wt, Literal, Literal-Wt (single-step no iteration), Prior (uses P(y|m_Lt), ignores utterances/dialog), and CRSA-Wt (CRSA with history-dependent lexicon). MDDial: classic RSA and Literal model. Note: no LLM-generation or prompting/chain-of-thought baselines; all baselines are RSA-family variants.",
183	      "metrics": "Reference game: listener task accuracy (argmax of listener posterior vs ground truth) and Information Gain = H_P(Y|M_Lt) - H_L(Y|U_t,M_Lt,W_t) per turn (bits gained from dialog history). MDDial: speaker perplexity (PPL) over uttered messages, and Task Success Rate (TSR = accuracy of final-turn listener diagnosis prediction).",
184	      "key_results": "Reference game (alpha=2.5, 500 rounds): CRSA outperforms all baselines on both accuracy (rising to ~1.0 by turn 4-5) and information gain; history-dependent lexicon (CRSA-Wt) neither helps nor hurts, indicating CRSA already captures that information; CRSA also reduces variance over turns. For alpha<=1 all iterative methods collapse to uniform. MDDial (Table 1): CRSA PPL 15.362 / TSR 0.085; classic RSA 15.395 / 0.085; Literal 60.835 / 0.079. CRSA marginally best but nearly identical to RSA because the doctor's estimate of the patient's belief stays near-uniform; overall task success is low, described as a hard setting needing more discriminative models / stronger LLM.",
185	      "code_link": "https://github.com/LautaroEst/crsa",
186	      "relevance": "Directly relevant conceptually: CRSA is an explicit, formal audience/Theory-of-Mind model where the speaker maintains and updates a belief B_{S,t}(m_Lt) over the listener's private mental state each turn and conditions utterance choice on it, which is exactly the \"simulate the audience per sentence\" intervention operationalized mathematically rather than in chain-of-thought. It offers a testable baseline framing, the MDDial dataset, and metrics (perplexity, task success). However it is NOT a writing-quality study: it uses fixed candidate-utterance sets (not free generation), a tiny 1B LLM, task-oriented dialog (not prose), and shows only marginal gains where audience belief is near-uniform, so it provides motivation and a formal contrast point but no direct evidence that in-context/CoT audience simulation improves writing quality."
187	    }
188	  ]
189	}

    {
      "file": "2601.06116_homogenization_problem_in_llms_diversity.pdf",
      "title": "The Homogenization Problem in LLMs: Towards Meaningful Diversity in AI Safety",
      "year": "2026",
      "key_contribution": "A conceptual/formal framework (grounded in queer theory) for characterizing \"homogenization\" — the collapse of LLM generations toward normative \"default\" modes — by modeling an LLM as a probability distribution over a tree of strings and scoring outputs against user-defined \"structures.\" Introduces \"xeno-reproduction\" as a class of diversity-promoting interventions and demonstrates the framework with a gender-bias case study on Claude 3.5 Haiku generating nurse love stories.",
      "methodology": "Formalizes an LLM as a distribution over a trie of token trajectories; each prefix indexes a subtree with its own distribution. Analysts define \"structures\" (scoring functions alpha: string -> [0,1]) that encode axes of difference; a \"system\" is a vector of structures, and the \"system barycenter\" (structure default) is the expected structure score over continuations from a prefix, estimated by Monte Carlo sampling of K continuations and scored by an LLM-as-judge ensemble. \"Deviance\"/\"orientation\" measure a trajectory's signed deviation from this default. Homogenization is formalized as barycenter entropy -> 0 plus expected-deviance and deviance-variance -> 0; xeno-reproduction reweights sampling (trajectory-level: P(y|x,w) ∝ P_base * exp(beta * deviance(y|x))) to push outputs away from the default subject to constraints. The case study branches a shared story trunk on gendered pronoun prefills to trace how the barycenter shifts.",
      "datasets": "No external benchmark dataset. Self-generated case-study corpus: 5 \"arms\" (root, trunk, branch_1 \"his partner\", branch_2 \"her partner\", branch_3 \"their partner\") x 201 trajectories each (200 sampled at temp=1.0 + 1 greedy) = ~1005 nurse love-story generations from Claude 3.5 Haiku (claude-3-5-haiku-20241022), max 512 tokens. Real-world reference proportions drawn from BLS CPS 2023, NCSBN 2024 nursing survey, Sabin et al. 2015, Gallup 2024.",
      "baselines": "No model/algorithm baselines in the empirical sense. The \"baseline\" is the unintervened LLM distribution (the root/unmarked prompt barycenter) compared against gender-marked branches and against a real-world statistical reference distribution. Appendix G maps the framework onto existing linguistic-diversity metrics (lexical n-gram, syntactic POS, semantic embedding, Vendi Score, MAUVE) as expressible special cases rather than experimental comparisons.",
      "metrics": "System barycenter (per-structure expected score, 4 structures: alpha_male, alpha_female, alpha_hetero, alpha_same-sex), expected deviance E[partial_n] (dimension-normalized L2 / RMS orientation), deviance variance Var[partial_n], barycenter entropy H (and effective number of structures exp(H) / Hill numbers), forking magnitude Delta_k, and a branch-probability estimator. Inter-judge agreement/calibration also reported.",
      "key_results": "Unprompted, Claude defaults to a female nurse (alpha_female≈0.73) in a heterosexual couple (alpha_hetero≈0.99), almost always named Sarah paired with Marcus; male and same-sex scores near zero. Marking \"her partner\" (branch_2) drives deviance to 0.035 (below root 0.319) — the most homogenizing arm. Only \"his partner\" (branch_1) substantially disrupts the default (deviance 0.622; male 0.867), and it triggers an implicit same-sex association (same-sex rises to 0.199) despite no same-sex prefill. Notably, the female-nurse bias is \"stronger than markedness\": ~9/200 branch_1 trajectories re-cast the nurse as female despite explicit \"his.\" Three-judge ensemble (Claude Opus, GPT-5, Gemini 2.5 Flash) agreed qualitatively but differed in calibration.",
      "code_link": "none found (no repository URL in paper; Appendix acknowledges Claude Code was used for the pipeline)",
      "relevance": "Only indirectly relevant. The paper is about diversity/bias homogenization, not audience-modeling, and it contains no ToM/audience-simulation intervention, so it does not test the hypothesis. However, it offers directly reusable experimental machinery for a writing-quality study: (1) an LLM-as-judge ensemble with a chain-of-thought scoring scaffold (enumerate characters/markers -> decide -> \"ANSWER: 0/1\"), three-judge soft scoring, and regex verdict parsing — a validated template for scoring generated writing along audience-relevant \"structures\"; (2) the tree/prefix \"forking token\" and per-sentence barycenter-tracking method (Appendix H measures the system default position-by-position along a generation), which is a concrete way to instrument writing sentence-by-sentence, matching the hypothesis's \"every sentence or so\" granularity; and (3) a homogenization/diversity metric (deviance, barycenter entropy, NoveltyBench-adjacent measures cited) that could quantify whether an audience-simulation prompt reduces annoying sameness. The nurse love-story generation setup and the semantic-similarity \"writing style from references\" structure (Appendix B.5, e.g. Gen-Z vs Boomer register) are reusable for building an audience-design writing task and evaluation."
    },
    {
      "file": "2211.08371_pragmatics_in_language_grounding_survey.pdf",

      "key_contribution": "A survey/position paper by Fried, Tomlin, Hu, Patel, and Nematzadeh arguing that LLMs lack direct access to the contexts that drive pragmatic (audience-sensitive) meaning and therefore fail at context-dependent communication.       "methodology": "This is a literature survey with no new algorithm or experiment. Concrete instantiations cited: RSA-augmented neural speakers/listeners (Monroe 2017; Andreas & Klein 2016; Cohn-Gordon 2018), pragmatic instruction generation/following (Fried 2018a,b), Liu 2023 (RSA + meta-learning with GPT in a reference game), FAIR 2022 (BART + planning in Diplomacy), Kojima 2021 (GPT-2 fine-tuned to adapt to human followers).",
      "metrics": "Recommends evaluating on two axes beyond corpus overlap: (1) task success — accuracy of a listener selecting the intended referent/carrying out the action; measured via communication-based evaluation / self-play, where a speaker's generation is fed to a separate listener model (Newman 2020), with a strong caveat to make the listener model dissimilar from the system and to use human evaluation when possible. (2) communicative efficiency/cost — time to task completion, utterance length and complexity (Effenberger 2021), lexical entrainment/adaptation over repeated interaction (Hawkins 2020), and human quality ratings (Kojima 2021). Explicit audience modeling via RSA has empirically improved reference games, instruction following/generation, captioning, summarization, MT, and dialogue. Stated limitations of RSA relevant to the hypothesis: it assumes a fixed known set of candidate utterances and, except for a few cases, is limited to single-turn interactions; multi-turn variants typically drop higher-order theory-of-mind to stay tractable.",
      "code_link": "none found (no artifact released; it is a survey. Individual surveyed systems e.g. Colors-in-Context, PhotoBook, CerealBar have their own public repos)",
      "relevance": "Strongly conceptually relevant, weakly empirically. The paper's core thesis is essentially the hypothesis: LLMs write/communicate poorly because they do not model the audience/context, and explicit audience-design (RSA-style: pick each utterance by simulating how the listener interprets it vs. alternatives) improves communication — reported to help across generation tasks. It supplies the theoretical vocabulary (audience design, common ground, RSA, communication-based/self-play evaluation, efficiency metrics) and directly usable evaluation designs: feed a model's sentence to a separate 'listener/audience' model and score comprehension + efficiency, using a dissimilar audience model, plus human ratings.     },
    {
      "file": "2507.14063_collaborative_rational_speech_act_multiturn_dialog.pdf",
      "title": "Collaborative Rational Speech Act: Pragmatic Reasoning for Multi-Turn Dialog",
      "year": "2025 (arXiv 2507.14063v2, Sep 2025)",
      "key_contribution": "Introduces Collaborative Rational Speech Act (CRSA), an information-theoretic extension of the Rational Speech Act framework that models multi-turn collaborative dialog by maximizing a per-turn gain function generalized from rate-distortion theory. content.text: I now have comprehensive notes. Let me add the two most directly-usable evaluation resources for this specific hypothesis: the Writing Quality benchmark/LAMP corpus (salesforce/creativity_eval) and the ALTPRAG pragmatics dataset.

input.command:
cd /workspaces/llms_don_t_do_much_tom_in_writ_20260714_234243_6b6ff0f4/code
GIT_LFS_SKIP_SMUDGE=1 git clone --depth 1 https://github.com/salesforce/creativity_eval.git 2>&1 | tail -3
cd /workspaces/llms_don_t_do_much_tom_in_writ_20260714_234243_6b6ff0f4
echo "=== creativity_eval tree ==="
find code/creativity_eval -maxdepth 2 -not -path '*/.git/*' | head -40
echo "=== size ==="; du -sh code/creativity_eval

content.text: Now let me write the datasets README.

input.content:
# Datasets

Resources for testing the hypothesis: *does having an LLM explicitly simulate the
audience in its chain-of-thought (roughly per sentence) improve its writing?*

Large data files are **not** committed to git (see `.gitignore`); small samples and
this README are. Follow the download instructions to reproduce locally.

The experiment naturally has two sides:
1. **Generation side** — writing tasks whose output quality we score with/without an
   audience-simulation CoT (WritingPrompts, LAMP, WQ benchmark).
2. **Diagnostic / audience-modeling side** — does the intervention actually improve
   the model's modeling of a reader's mental state? (ALTPRAG, ToMBench, FANToM, BigToM).

---

## 1. WritingPrompts (open-ended creative writing) — DOWNLOADED SAMPLE

- **Source**: HuggingFace `euclaise/writingprompts` (originally Fan et al. 2018, the
  Reddit r/WritingPrompts corpus).
- **Location**: `datasets/writingprompts_sample/train_300.json` (300-row sample, ~1 MB).
- **Format**: JSON list of `{"prompt": str, "story": str}`.
- **Task**: open-ended story generation from a one-line prompt. Good for measuring
  writing quality / homogenization and for defining explicit target audiences.
- **Why relevant**: a clean, audience-agnostic generation task. The intervention can
  add a target reader ("write for a tired parent" / "for a domain expert") and we test
  whether per-sentence audience simulation changes quality & appropriateness.

### Download (full set)
```python
from datasets import load_dataset
ds = load_dataset("euclaise/writingprompts")          # train/validation/test
ds.save_to_disk("datasets/writingprompts_full")
```
### Load the sample
```python
import json
data = json.load(open("datasets/writingprompts_sample/train_300.json"))
```

---

## 2. ALTPRAG — pragmatic intent inference (audience design diagnostic) — DOWNLOADED

- **Source**: HuggingFace `Huangtubaye233/AltPrag` (Bao et al. 2025, arXiv 2505.18497).
- **Location**: `datasets/altprag/altprag_test.jsonl` (1,298 rows, ~1.8 MB) +
  `datasets/altprag/samples.json`.
- **Format**: JSONL. Columns: `context`, `root` (initial utterance),
  `candidate_sentence_1/2` (two pragmatically distinct continuations),
  `candidate_sentence_1/2_intention` (reference intent explanation),
  `human_annotation_sentence_1/2_GM` (Gricean maxim flouted, or None).
- **Task**: given a context and two equally-valid-but-pragmatically-different replies,
  infer the speaker's intent / which Gricean maxim is flouted.
- **Why relevant**: a human-verified, scored measure of *audience design / speaker-intent
  reasoning*. Directly usable to check whether an audience-simulation CoT improves the
  model's pragmatic reasoning (measures the mechanism the hypothesis proposes). Paired
  with the paper's 10-point LLM-as-judge rubric + pairwise win-rate protocol.

### Download
```python
from datasets import load_dataset
ds = load_dataset("Huangtubaye233/AltPrag", split="test")   # only a 'test' split exists
ds.to_json("datasets/altprag/altprag_test.jsonl")
```

---

## 3. Writing Quality (WQ) Benchmark + LAMP corpus — CLONED (in `code/`)

- **Source**: `salesforce/creativity_eval` repo (Chakrabarty et al., "AI-Slop to
  AI-Polish?", COLM 2025, arXiv 2504.07532). Already cloned — see `code/README.md`.
- **Location**:
  - `code/creativity_eval/WritingRewards/WQ-benchmark-data/*.json` — 4,729 pairwise
    writing-quality judgments across 5 sub-datasets (Art-or-Artifice, LAMP-test,
    Style-Mimic, Synthetic-Mirror, LM-Arena). **Full JSON present (not LFS).**
  - `code/creativity_eval/Writing_Alignment/LAMP/LAMP.json` — 1,282
    `<AI-generated, expert-MFA-edited>` paragraph pairs with 1-10 quality scores.
  - `code/creativity_eval/WritingRewards/WQRM_annotations.json` — reward-model annotations.
- **Task / why relevant**: the single most on-point *writing-quality* resource. SOTA
  LLMs (incl. o1/R1 reasoning models) score **near chance** on WQ, and generic CoT does
  **not** help — so it is the ideal testbed to see whether an *audience-simulation* CoT
  beats plain CoT. LAMP supplies expert-edit "gold" revisions and the WQRM reward model
  gives an automatic quality score (74% agreement with experts).
- **Note**: model checkpoints (WQRM, edit model) are Git-LFS and were **not** pulled
  (`GIT_LFS_SKIP_SMUDGE=1`). Pull with `git lfs pull` inside the repo if the reward model
  weights are needed; the JSON data above is sufficient for evaluation-by-LLM-judge.

---

## 4. ToM benchmarks (audience/mental-state modeling diagnostics) — CLONED (in `code/`)

These measure whether the intervention improves the model's Theory-of-Mind, i.e. the
underlying capacity the hypothesis says is missing. All are **evaluation-only** (do not
train on them — contamination risk).

- **ToMBench** — `code/ToMBench/data/*.jsonl`: 2,860 bilingual multiple-choice items,
  8 tasks / 31 social-cognition abilities (Chen et al., ACL 2024, arXiv 2402.15052).
- **FANToM** — `code/fantom/` (+ `code/thought-tracing/data/fantom/fantom_v1.2.json`):
  info-asymmetric multi-party conversation ToM; a character leaves and rejoins unaware
  of what was said. Directly about *audience knowledge asymmetry* (Kim et al., EMNLP 2023,
  arXiv 2310.15421).
- **BigToM** — `code/thought-tracing/data/bigtom/bigtom_agree90.json`: causal-template
  false-belief reasoning (Gandhi et al. 2023).
- **ToMi (paraphrased)** — `code/thought-tracing/data/paraphrased_tomi/`: Sally-Anne
  style first/second-order false-belief QA.
- **MMToM-QA** — `code/thought-tracing/data/mmtom_qa/revised_questions.jsonl`.

---

## Optional / not downloaded (pointers for the experiment runner)

- **PercepToM benchmarks** (Percept-ToMi, Percept-FANToM; arXiv 2407.06004) — per-sentence
  perception-annotation ToM; closest comprehension analog to "simulate the audience per
  sentence." Released CC BY-NC 4.0 (check the paper page for the release URL).
- **PhotoBook / Takmaz-2020 referring expressions** (arXiv 2305.19933) — audience-aware
  referential game; code at `github.com/nicofirst1/speaker-adaptation`.
- **UKPConvArg1 + Cornell Movie-Quotes** (arXiv 2301.10283) — audience-preference pairwise
  data; code+data at `github.com/CrowdDynamicsLab/StyleInfusion`.
- **EmpatheticDialogues / PersuasionForGood** (arXiv 2501.15355) — turn-level ToM dialogue.

## Recommended primary datasets for the experiment
1. **WritingPrompts** (+ author-defined audiences) — generation task.
2. **WQ benchmark / LAMP** — automatic + human writing-quality scoring.
3. **ALTPRAG** — diagnostic that the intervention improves pragmatic/audience reasoning.
4. **FANToM** — diagnostic under explicit audience knowledge-asymmetry.

1. **Generation side** — writing tasks whose output quality we score with/without an
   audience-simulation CoT (WritingPrompts, LAMP, WQ benchmark).
2. **Diagnostic / audience-modeling side** — does the intervention actually improve
   the model's modeling of a reader's mental state? (ALTPRAG, ToMBench, FANToM, BigToM).

---

## 1. WritingPrompts (open-ended creative writing) — DOWNLOADED SAMPLE

- **Source**: HuggingFace `euclaise/writingprompts` (originally Fan et al. 2018, the
  Reddit r/WritingPrompts corpus).
- **Location**: `datasets/writingprompts_sample/train_300.json` (300-row sample, ~1 MB).
- **Format**: JSON list of `{"prompt": str, "story": str}`.
- **Task**: open-ended story generation from a one-line prompt. Good for measuring
  writing quality / homogenization and for defining explicit target audiences.
- **Why relevant**: a clean, audience-agnostic generation task. The intervention can
  add a target reader ("write for a tired parent" / "for a domain expert") and we test
  whether per-sentence audience simulation changes quality & appropriateness.

### Download (full set)
```python
from datasets import load_dataset
ds = load_dataset("euclaise/writingprompts")          # train/validation/test
ds.save_to_disk("datasets/writingprompts_full")
```
### Load the sample
```python
import json
data = json.load(open("datasets/writingprompts_sample/train_300.json"))
```

---

## 2. ALTPRAG — pragmatic intent inference (audience design diagnostic) — DOWNLOADED

- **Source**: HuggingFace `Huangtubaye233/AltPrag` (Bao et al. 2025, arXiv 2505.18497).
- **Location**: `datasets/altprag/altprag_test.jsonl` (1,298 rows, ~1.8 MB) +
  `datasets/altprag/samples.json`.
- **Format**: JSONL. Columns: `context`, `root` (initial utterance),
  `candidate_sentence_1/2` (two pragmatically distinct continuations),
  `candidate_sentence_1/2_intention` (reference intent explanation),
  `human_annotation_sentence_1/2_GM` (Gricean maxim flouted, or None).
- **Task**: given a context and two equally-valid-but-pragmatically-different replies,
  infer the speaker's intent / which Gricean maxim is flouted.
- **Why relevant**: a human-verified, scored measure of *audience design / speaker-intent
  reasoning*. Directly usable to check whether an audience-simulation CoT improves the
  model's pragmatic reasoning (measures the mechanism the hypothesis proposes). Paired
  with the paper's 10-point LLM-as-judge rubric + pairwise win-rate protocol.

### Download
```python
from datasets import load_dataset
ds = load_dataset("Huangtubaye233/AltPrag", split="test")   # only a 'test' split exists
ds.to_json("datasets/altprag/altprag_test.jsonl")
```

---

## 3. Writing Quality (WQ) Benchmark + LAMP corpus — CLONED (in `code/`)

- **Source**: `salesforce/creativity_eval` repo (Chakrabarty et al., "AI-Slop to
  AI-Polish?", COLM 2025, arXiv 2504.07532). Already cloned — see `code/README.md`.
- **Location**:
  - `code/creativity_eval/WritingRewards/WQ-benchmark-data/*.json` — 4,729 pairwise
    writing-quality judgments across 5 sub-datasets (Art-or-Artifice, LAMP-test,
    Style-Mimic, Synthetic-Mirror, LM-Arena). **Full JSON present (not LFS).**
  - `code/creativity_eval/Writing_Alignment/LAMP/LAMP.json` — 1,282
    `<AI-generated, expert-MFA-edited>` paragraph pairs with 1-10 quality scores.
  - `code/creativity_eval/WritingRewards/WQRM_annotations.json` — reward-model annotations.
- **Task / why relevant**: the single most on-point *writing-quality* resource. SOTA
  LLMs (incl. o1/R1 reasoning models) score **near chance** on WQ, and generic CoT does
  **not** help — so it is the ideal testbed to see whether an *audience-simulation* CoT
  beats plain CoT. LAMP supplies expert-edit "gold" revisions and the WQRM reward model
  gives an automatic quality score (74% agreement with experts).
- **Note**: model checkpoints (WQRM, edit model) are Git-LFS and were **not** pulled
  (`GIT_LFS_SKIP_SMUDGE=1`). Pull with `git lfs pull` inside the repo if the reward model
  weights are needed; the JSON data above is sufficient for evaluation-by-LLM-judge.

---

## 4. ToM benchmarks (audience/mental-state modeling diagnostics) — CLONED (in `code/`)

These measure whether the intervention improves the model's Theory-of-Mind, i.e. the
underlying capacity the hypothesis says is missing. All are **evaluation-only** (do not
train on them — contamination risk).

- **ToMBench** — `code/ToMBench/data/*.jsonl`: 2,860 bilingual multiple-choice items,
  8 tasks / 31 social-cognition abilities (Chen et al., ACL 2024, arXiv 2402.15052).
- **FANToM** — `code/fantom/` (+ `code/thought-tracing/data/fantom/fantom_v1.2.json`):
  info-asymmetric multi-party conversation ToM; a character leaves and rejoins unaware
  of what was said. Directly about *audience knowledge asymmetry* (Kim et al., EMNLP 2023,
  arXiv 2310.15421).
- **BigToM** — `code/thought-tracing/data/bigtom/bigtom_agree90.json`: causal-template
  false-belief reasoning (Gandhi et al. 2023).
- **ToMi (paraphrased)** — `code/thought-tracing/data/paraphrased_tomi/`: Sally-Anne
  style first/second-order false-belief QA.
- **MMToM-QA** — `code/thought-tracing/data/mmtom_qa/revised_questions.jsonl`.

---

## Optional / not downloaded (pointers for the experiment runner)

- **PercepToM benchmarks** (Percept-ToMi, Percept-FANToM; arXiv 2407.06004) — per-sentence
  perception-annotation ToM; closest comprehension analog to "simulate the audience per
  sentence." Released CC BY-NC 4.0 (check the paper page for the release URL).
- **PhotoBook / Takmaz-2020 referring expressions** (arXiv 2305.19933) — audience-aware
  referential game; code at `github.com/nicofirst1/speaker-adaptation`.
- **UKPConvArg1 + Cornell Movie-Quotes** (arXiv 2301.10283) — audience-preference pairwise
  data; code+data at `github.com/CrowdDynamicsLab/StyleInfusion`.
- **EmpatheticDialogues / PersuasionForGood** (arXiv 2501.15355) — turn-level ToM dialogue.

## Recommended primary datasets for the experiment
1. **WritingPrompts** (+ author-defined audiences) — generation task.
2. **WQ benchmark / LAMP** — automatic + human writing-quality scoring.
3. **ALTPRAG** — diagnostic that the intervention improves pragmatic/audience reasoning.
4. **FANToM** — diagnostic under explicit audience knowledge-asymmetry.

content.text: Now the papers README with all metadata:

input.content:
# Downloaded Papers

14 papers on Theory of Mind (ToM), audience-aware / pragmatic generation, and LLM
writing quality — gathered for the hypothesis that having an LLM explicitly simulate the
audience in its chain-of-thought (per sentence) improves its writing.

Grouped by role. ★ = closest to the hypothesis / deep-read priority.

## A. Audience-aware / pragmatic generation (the closest prior work)

1. ★ **Speaking the Language of Your Listener: Audience-Aware Adaptation via Plug-and-Play Theory of Mind**
   — `2305.19933_speaking_language_of_listener_audience_aware_ToM.pdf`
   - Takmaz, Brandizzi, Giulianelli, Pezzelle, Fernández — ACL Findings 2023 — arXiv:2305.19933
   - Speaker adapts referring expressions by a simulator (ToM) predicting listener success;
     steers a frozen LM at decode time (plug-and-play, no fine-tune).
   - **Audience-aware > self-aware > baseline** on listener resolution (71.8/26.7% IND/OOD).
   - Code: https://github.com/nicofirst1/speaker-adaptation
   - Why: the paper closest to the hypothesis, but steers via hidden-state gradients, not CoT.

2. ★ **Reasoning about Pragmatics with Neural Listeners and Speakers**
   — `1604.00562_reasoning_about_pragmatics_neural_listeners_speakers.pdf`
   - Andreas & Klein — EMNLP 2016 — arXiv:1604.00562
   - Seminal neural RSA: sample from literal speaker, rerank by a simulated listener.
     +17% accuracy on reference game; tiny speaker weight preserves fluency.
   - Code: https://github.com/jacobandreas/pragma
   - Why: per-utterance "simulate the audience then pick" — the discrete analog of the intervention.

3. **Audience-Centric Natural Language Generation via Style Infusion**
   — `2301.10283_audience_centric_NLG_style_infusion.pdf`
   - Kumar et al. — EMNLP Findings 2023 — arXiv:2301.10283
   - Audience preference as an external BERT discriminator reward on GPT-2 (persuasive/memorable).
   - Code+data: https://github.com/CrowdDynamicsLab/StyleInfusion
   - Why: a non-CoT, non-ToM audience-modeling baseline + linguistic-feature eval suite.

4. **Pragmatic Reasoning Improves LLM Code Generation (CodeRSA)**
   — `2502.15835_pragmatic_reasoning_improves_llm_code_generation.pdf`
   - arXiv:2502.15835 (2025) — RSA-inspired execution-free reranker: reverse-generate what
     each candidate "says" to the audience, pick the distinctively-supported one.
   - Best in 10/12 settings. Code: none found in paper.
   - Why: operationalizes audience modeling as reverse-inference + rerank (reusable idea for prose).

5. **Collaborative Rational Speech Act: Pragmatic Reasoning for Multi-Turn Dialog (CRSA)**
   — `2507.14063_collaborative_rational_speech_act_multiturn_dialog.pdf`
   - arXiv:2507.14063 (2025) — information-theoretic RSA where the speaker maintains a belief
     over the listener's private meaning each turn. Code: https://github.com/LautaroEst/crsa
   - Why: formal "simulate the audience per turn"; marginal gains where audience belief ~uniform.

6. **Pragmatics in Language Grounding: Phenomena, Tasks, and Modeling Approaches** (survey)
   — `2211.08371_pragmatics_in_language_grounding_survey.pdf`
   - Fried, Tomlin, Hu, Patel, Nematzadeh — arXiv:2211.08371 (2023)
   - Thesis ≈ the hypothesis: LLMs communicate poorly because they don't model audience/context;
     RSA-style audience design helps. Recommends communication-based / self-play evaluation.

## B. ToM-aware generation with reflection/perspective (method analogs)

7. ★ **ToM-agent: LLMs as Theory of Mind Aware Generative Agents with Counterfactual Reflection**
   — `2501.15355_ToM_agent_generative_agents_counterfactual_reflection.pdf`
   - arXiv:2501.15355 (2025) — agent infers partner's Belief-Desire-Intention + confidence,
     predicts their reply, reflects on the gap. Improves dialogue success (SR@t up, turns down).
   - Code: promised post-acceptance, none found. Data: EmpatheticDialogues, PersuasionForGood.
   - Why: "predict the reader's reaction then reflect" — turn-level version of the intervention.

8. ★ **Perceptions to Beliefs: Precursory Inferences for Theory of Mind in LLMs (PercepToM)**
   — `2407.06004_perceptions_to_beliefs_precursory_inferences_tom.pdf`
   - arXiv:2407.06004 (2024) — per-sentence: prompt the LLM to tag who perceived each sentence,
     filter to the target's perspective, then answer. Big false-belief gains.
   - Why: the *per-sentence* "simulate whose perspective sees this" mechanism, for QA.

## C. Does default LLM ToM even work? (motivation + cautions)

9. **Large Language Models Fail on Trivial Alterations to Theory-of-Mind Tasks**
   — `2302.08399_llms_fail_trivial_alterations_tom_tasks.pdf` — Ullman — arXiv:2302.08399 (2023)
   - ToM-preserving perturbations flip GPT-3.5 to wrong beliefs → default ToM is brittle.

10. **Clever Hans or Neural Theory of Mind? Stress Testing Social Reasoning in LLMs**
    — `2305.14763_clever_hans_or_neural_tom_stress_testing.pdf` — Shapira et al. — arXiv:2305.14763
    - 15 LLMs × 6 ToM tasks; adversarial variants collapse performance. **Warns CoT can inflate
      results via shortcuts** → need adversarial/true-belief controls. Code: github.com/salavi/Clever_Hans_or_N-ToM

11. **A Survey of Theory of Mind in LLMs: Evaluations, Representations, and Safety Risks**
    — `2502.06470_survey_theory_of_mind_in_llms.pdf` — AAAI 2025 — arXiv:2502.06470
    - LLM ToM is real but non-robust; perspective-taking prompting ("Think Twice") helps.

12. **The Pragmatic Mind of Machines: Tracing the Emergence of Pragmatic Competence (ALTPRAG)**
    — `2505.18497_pragmatic_mind_of_machines_emergence.pdf` — arXiv:2505.18497 (2025)
    - Pragmatic competence rises base→SFT→DPO. Introduces **ALTPRAG** (downloaded) + a
      human-validated 10-point rubric / pairwise-win LLM-judge protocol.

## D. Writing quality & homogenization (the evaluation target)

13. ★ **AI-Slop to AI-Polish? Aligning LMs through Edit-Based Writing Rewards & Test-time Compute**
    — `2504.07532_ai_slop_to_ai_polish_edit_based_writing_rewards.pdf` — COLM 2025 — arXiv:2504.07532
    - **WQ benchmark** (4,729 pairs; SOTA LLMs ~chance), **WQRM** reward model (74%), **LAMP**
      expert-edit corpus, edit+best-of-N pipeline. **Generic CoT (o1/R1) does NOT help writing.**
    - Code+data: https://github.com/salesforce/creativity_eval (cloned).
    - Why: rigorous writing-quality testbed + strong negative baseline for "just add reasoning."

14. **The Homogenization Problem in LLMs: Towards Meaningful Diversity in AI Safety**
    — `2601.06116_homogenization_problem_in_llms_diversity.pdf` — arXiv:2601.06116 (2026)
    - Formalizes homogenization (collapse to a "default"); LLM-judge ensemble + per-sentence
      barycenter tracking + deviance/diversity metrics reusable to measure "annoying sameness."

See `../literature_review.md` for full synthesis and `../resources.md` for the catalog.

=== thought-tracing README head === # 🚲 Thought Tracing This is the official repository of our 2025 COLM paper:
"Thought Tracing: Hypothesis-Driven Theory-of-Mind Reasoning for Large Language Models" Please cite our work if you found the resources in this repository useful: ```bib @inproceedings{kim2025tracing, title={Hypothesis-Driven Theory-of-Mind Reasoning for Large Language Models}, author={Hyunwoo Kim and Melanie Sclar and Tan Zhi-Xuan and Lance Ying and Sydney Levine and Yang Liu and Joshua B. Tenenbaum and Yejin Choi}, booktitle={COLM}, year=2025 } ``` ## Environment setup ```bash conda env create -f environment.yml; conda activate thought-tracing pip install flash-attn==2.5.1.post1 python -m ipykernel install --user --name thought-tracing --display-name "thought-tracing" huggingface-cli login ``` ### Adding your own agent All you need to do is create an agent class with the method `interact()` or `batch_interact()`. ## Running Evaluation ### ToMi ``` cd revised_tomi python test_tomi.py --model gpt-4o-2024-11-20 --use-tracing --tracing-model gpt-4o-2024-11-20 --print --run-id tracer-first-run --dataset tomi --tracer-type tracer ``` ### FANToM ``` cd fantom python test_fantom.py --model gpt-4o-2024-08-06 --use-tracing --tracing-model gpt-4o-2024-08-06 --print --dataset fantom --tracer-type multi-tracer --input-is-chat --run-id tracer-first-run === thought-tracing entrypoints === code/thought-tracing/agents: base.py gemini.py gpt.py load_model.py together_ai.py vllm.py code/thought-tracing/prompt_templates: label_actions_bigtom.txt

input.content:
# Cloned Repositories

Code + bundled data for baselines, ToM evaluation, and writing-quality evaluation.
No user-specified `code_references` were given in the topic spec, so these were selected
for relevance to the hypothesis (audience-simulation-in-CoT improves writing). All cloned
`--depth 1` (shallow).

---

## 1. `thought-tracing/` — Hypothesis-Driven ToM Reasoning (method analog) ★
- **URL**: https://github.com/skywalker023/thought-tracing (COLM 2025, arXiv:2502.11881)
- **Purpose**: Inference-time ToM reasoning — the model traces/updates hypotheses about a
  character's mental state step by step (a "tracer" agent) before answering. This is the
  **closest existing mechanism to the hypothesis**: explicit, verbalized, incremental
  mental-state simulation added to a frozen LLM. Swapping "character" → "reader/audience"
  and running it during *generation* is essentially the proposed intervention.
- **Key files**: `hypothesis.py`, `agents/{gpt,gemini,vllm,together_ai}.py`,
  `prompt_templates/*.txt`, `bigtom/test_bigtom.py`, `fantom/test_fantom.py`,
  `mmtom_qa/test_mmtom.py`, `revised_tomi/`.
- **Bundled data** (evaluation-only): `data/fantom/fantom_v1.2.json`,
  `data/bigtom/bigtom_agree90.json`, `data/paraphrased_tomi/`, `data/mmtom_qa/`.
- **Deps**: `environment.yml` (conda) — API keys or vLLM; adaptable to the modal-vllm skill.
- **Entry point**: e.g. `python fantom/test_fantom.py --model gpt-4o --use-tracing --tracer-type multi-tracer`.

## 2. `creativity_eval/` — Writing-quality evaluation (WQ / WQRM / LAMP) ★
- **URL**: https://github.com/salesforce/creativity_eval ("AI-Slop to AI-Polish?", COLM 2025, arXiv:2504.07532)
- **Purpose**: the primary **writing-quality testbed**. Provides the WQ benchmark, the WQRM
  reward model, the LAMP expert-edit corpus, and the edit + best-of-N pipeline.
- **Key files / data**:
  - `WritingRewards/WQ-benchmark-data/*.json` — 4,729 pairwise quality judgments (full JSON present).
  - `WritingRewards/WQRM_inference.py`, `WritingRewards/WQRM_annotations.json` — reward model.
  - `Writing_Alignment/LAMP/LAMP.json` — 1,282 AI-draft → expert-edited pairs with 1-10 scores.
  - `Writing_Alignment/{utils_generate_edits.py, prompts/}` — the 3-step edit pipeline.
  - `Art_or_Artifice/run_llm_eval.py` — LLM-judge writing eval harness.
- **Note**: cloned with `GIT_LFS_SKIP_SMUDGE=1`, so **model weights (LFS) were not pulled**;
  the JSON datasets and Python harnesses are present. Run `git lfs pull` inside the repo
  only if the WQRM/edit-model checkpoints are needed.

## 3. `ToMBench/` — Theory-of-Mind benchmark (diagnostic)
- **URL**: https://github.com/zhchen18/ToMBench (ACL 2024, arXiv:2402.15052)
- **Purpose**: bilingual multiple-choice ToM eval, 2,860 items across 8 tasks / 31 abilities.
- **Data**: `data/*.jsonl` (per-task). **Evaluation only — do not train on it.**

## 4. `fantom/` — FANToM conversational ToM (audience-asymmetry diagnostic)
- **URL**: https://github.com/skywalker023/fantom (EMNLP 2023, arXiv:2310.15421)
- **Purpose**: stress-tests ToM under information asymmetry in multi-party conversation
  (a character leaves, misses info, rejoins) — a direct probe of *audience knowledge modeling*.

  (also bundled in `thought-tracing/data/fantom/`).

---

## Related repos NOT cloned (pointers)
- `github.com/nicofirst1/speaker-adaptation` — plug-and-play ToM speaker (arXiv:2305.19933).
- `github.com/jacobandreas/pragma` — neural RSA listener/speaker (arXiv:1604.00562).
- `github.com/CrowdDynamicsLab/StyleInfusion` — audience-centric style infusion (arXiv:2301.10283).
- `github.com/LautaroEst/crsa` — Collaborative RSA multi-turn (arXiv:2507.14063).
- `github.com/salavi/Clever_Hans_or_N-ToM` — adversarial ToM stress tests (arXiv:2305.14763).

## How these support the experiment
- **Intervention scaffold**: adapt `thought-tracing` prompt/agent structure to run a
  per-sentence "simulate the reader" tracer during generation.
- **Baselines**: plain generation, plain CoT, perspective-taking (SimToM / "Think Twice"),
  self-refine/edit (creativity_eval edit pipeline), RSA-style rerank.
- **Writing-quality scoring**: `creativity_eval` WQRM + LLM-judge + LAMP expert edits.
- **Mechanism diagnostics**: ALTPRAG (in `datasets/`), FANToM, ToMBench, BigToM.
